rating 5.0. 1975 Elvis and his last formal studio sessions for RCA Elvis Today was originally issued in May 1975, and with just 10 cuts felt kind of lite! However, when you weigh the material and how it holds up 40 Years later, it's an amazing release... Promised Land was the first release of 1975 and it was the best of the Stax material from 1973, Elvis had not one Recording session inside a studio in 74, though a live concert was recorded and chopped down by rca for an in person album from memphis, so 1975 was to be a new start... 74 had ups and downs, Elvis was basically relegated to the country charts, rock and pop refused to play his newest works....
March 75 he recorded what would be the Today album and he dug in to make a great country album, he chose some great material from his teenage years, paired it with some great covers, and some new material....the lead off cut T-R-O-U-B-L-E is a Jerry Chesnutt number about a guy who lives vicariously through his music, watching the action from a stage playing piano, Elvis rocked this number perfectly, AND I LOVE YOU SO is a mournful kind of love song that speaks of loneliness and something with which Elvis could identify, SUSAN WHEN SHE TRIED, almost mirrors the Statler Brothers version, WOMAN WITHOUT LOVE a song Elvis could really work out on emotionally.....
SHAKE A HAND from 1953 and a song I'm very sure he sang for years
Pieces of my life, stories that could have been pages in his diary......
Needless to say you can't go wrong on this album.......

input:
rating 5.0. Truly Historic This volume of the Beatles Anthology continues where volume 1 left off, around the time of Help. It also presents another new song, "Real Love." This was a time of creativity and a change in direction in the Beatles music. In some cases, the alternate takes presented here are radicaly different from the masters. One example of this is "Got to Get You Into My Life." There are also some live tracks on disc 1, which are very interesting to hear. Some highlights for me are:

*Everybody's Trying To Be My Baby - from the 1965 Shea Stadium concert, not used in the film.

*I'm Looking Through You - different, with a very funky, earthy sound.

*Rock and Roll Music - live in Japan 1966, the final tour.

*Good Morning Good Morning - without the chorus or overdubs

I greatly enjoyed hearing the working versions of the Sgt. Pepper and Magical Mystery Tour, two of their most creative albums. This album is a must for the serious collector.

rating 5.0. the sound of brilliant art being made Of course I have heard the songs on the albums many times. But it's amazing to hear the Beatles--sometimes just John or Paul--creating a song in a studio at a different rhythm, a different vocalizing, a different feel. It's a reminder that what is on the albums came from hard work, doing the songs over and over, trying them out in different ways, stretching for the way to do it that best captured their group sense. The anthologies are a great complement to Geoff Emerick's book about recording the Beatles. After reading that book, one understands better that it may seem effortless on the albums but it was created by the intersection of determination, grinding effort, and remarkable musical ability. The casual way Emerick writes about "the boys and George Martin felt take 34 was the best" serves to remind one of what it means to get it right. And they did, so many times.

The live tracks are a real bonus. Much of the time the group sounds a bit shaky, rushed, as if they were trying to stay in sync and not able to hear one another...which is the stated reason they stopped touring. With today's in-ear monitors, they probably would still be playing (look at the Who, still touring 40 years later), but it was 1965 and thousands of screaming girls made it impossible for the musicians to hear their stage sound. They did what they could until it wasn't worth it. And, of course, there is the priceless sound of John thanking Ringo for his singing of "Yesterday" after Paul does a spot-on live solo effort. The boys may have found this funny but it sure seems snarky in retrospect.

rating 5.0. Yes they(except for Ringo) were musical geniuses I was a fan in the late 60s and saw recently Paul at Citifield in NYC. George Martin enhanced their work with his editing and arrangements, but the Beatles were each and together extraordinary musicians and live performances proved that. For those who say they aint Mozart, true, but they were as equal to beethoven. Here is why:
John said rollover Beethoven. Paul's Eleanor Rigby was the answer to that. I like Real Love even more. Leonard bernstein of the NY Philharmonic said they would be listened to for hundreds of years when they were still together. Phil Spectre collaborated with them and Billy Joel paid tribute and was as versatile on the piano as Paul. Billy said most music today is recycled melodies and compositions written long ago. The prolific Beatles definitely broke their own path, by the sheer youthful burst of energy and self confidence. George, influencing so many other musicians and inventing new sounds(psychodelic or world music) was one of the top rated guitarists and sitarists in the world, with a gifted quick to learn ear. John's words and Paul's composing is etched into my soul and makes me say Let's dance, as Donna Sumner said, or meditate.

rating 5.0. Beatles! Items arrived on time, and in very good condition. I always enjoy buying used from Amazon, because you save alot of $$$$ over brand-new, and I was not let down! Buying new-conditon items at used prices is always a good thing. :-)

But the full songs on it are ok, so I gave it 3 stars.  With the size of our music collection, espeically the Beatles, I doubt we'd listen to this one again.  We have much better ones of theirs to listen to.  Unless you just want to own everything that says Beatles on it, I wouldn't really recommend this.

rating 5.0. The Creative Beatles For anyone with an interest in western music. Anthology 2 shows the creative Beatles, raw and unfiltered, in their phenomenally creative middle period. Lennon and McCartney are at the absolute peak of their powers. Some of the studio outtakes are better than the released versions - or at the least worthy alternatives. I think these disks will go down in history as a revealing snap shot of the greatest band ever at work.

rating 5.0. Please see my review of Anthology 1 The Beatles were a rock group that made it very big. They were pretty ordinary when they started out, but they worked long and hard, learning all the while, fought the odds and stayed together long enough to become truly great. Because the public usually only gets to hear the finished, polished products, you might come to believe that it didn't involve that much effort - but it did, and here's some of that effort captured as it was expended. You hear the false starts, the strain on young vocal cords, the frustration, and somehow, through it all, the inspiration and final, excellent execution. Because teenage girls drowned them out with screaming, and because they initially wore silly haircuts, it's easy to assume that they were just another fad. But, you would be wrong.

rating 4.0. They are a nice supplement to their original releases but will never replace ... This album is probably not for casual Beatles fans.  These Anthology albums are alternative takes, early demos, false starts etc. They are a nice supplement to their original releases but will never replace them.  On Anthology 2 you hear the development of their songs between 1966-1967, with disc 2 focusing on Sgt. Peppers & Magical Mystery Tour songs.  It's fun to hear the Beatles try different arrangements on familiar songs as they work toward the versions we are all familiar with.  A worthwhile purchase for Beatles fans.

input: rating 4.0. Great collection but one little flaw. Very good compilation but Sony messed up on the second disc.  Each disc is fairly chronological in terms of when the recordings were made with disc one containing primarily 50's recordings, disc two, 60s and 70s recordings and disc 3 from the 70s forward.  On disc two they include the song "What Are You Doing The Rest of Your Life" which was originally recorded by Mr. Mathis on his Love Story album.  However, the track is actually his second recording of the song from the album How Do You Keep the Music Playing from 1993.  It would have been better to have included the first recording on disc 2 because it is a stark contrast from the early 70's recordings to jump to a 1993 recording then back to 70s recordings.  Also, the song follows the title song from the album Love Story and would have been consistent in terms of arrangement and musical instrumentation background and would have provided a much smoother presentation.  I wish Sony would reissue his 70s albums on CD.

No England Swings, Winchester, etc.  very disappointed, since I already have her hits & wanted songs from her albums.

rating 5.0. Great cd of Petula covering hits by other artists.  Contains none of her hits, but a lot of rare covers. There are a multitude of inexpensive cds available covering Petula’s hit 45’s from the 60’s & 70’s.  However, for music lovers trying to find copies of non-45’s songs from her albums, especially covers of then-current hits, the problem is substantial.  The few available box sets with a representative sample are really expensive & well outside my budget & the few individual albums available are too expensive.  I was overjoyed to find this relatively inexpensive cd with a wonderful sampling of those songs:  elusive butterfly, going out of my head, homeward bound, etc.  A terrific choice if looking to expand your Petula collection at a reasonable price.

rating 5.0. Wonderful tribute to a classic album I have purchased quite a few Mojo compilations over the years and I would rate most as average, at best.  This cd is the exception.  Songs are very well done in a way that honors the greatness of the original versions.  For lovers of Cohen’s first album this is a worthwhile purchase.

rating 5.0. Terrific career overview Excellent overview of the work of one of my favorite pop groups of the 60’s.  Harmonies are so lovely in all their work.  Too bad this item is so hard to purchase at a reasonable price.

input:
rating 5.0. A Must-Have for Beatles Collectors Of the three Beatles Anthology volumes, this first one is easily the most exciting for Beatles fans and collectors. The idea here is to give a new spin to the old classics, and present alternate takes, working versions, live renditions, all of which are being released here for the first time.

This volume takes fans back to the very beginning, to 1958's "That'll Be The Day." What a thrill. Then we hear home demos that were recorded in Paul's garage. This is followed by the material recorded in Hamburg with Tony Sheridan. We are treated to five selections from the Beatles' Decca Audition. Even if it wasn't the greatest tape the Beatles could've made, I'm sure Decca is stil kicking themselves now for not signing the Beatles!

It gets better and better as time goes by. The Beatles sang "I'll Get You" at the London Palladium in 1963, and "All My Loving" on Ed Sullivan in 1964. Both of those recordings are presented here. Other highlights include:

*One After 909 - I actually prefer this version to the one that got released in 1970.

*Eight Days a Week - the tight harmony at the beginning works well. Also the false starts were interesting.

*How Do You Do It - the unreleased single.

*Free As A Bird - a new Beatles masterpiece.

Die-hard Beatles fans will not be disappointed with this set!
rating 5.0. FABULOUS Takes us back to the early years,with their early hits,and  their versions during rehearsals,recording sessions to the master recordings

rating 5.0. Great gift! I got all 3 Beatles Anthology CDs for a family member for the holidays and she loved them! They're great collections that include rare recordings as well as well-known Beatles favorites. The whole family got a kick out of listening to the albums in the car. The booklets contain many wonderful images as well as other great info about the band.

rating 4.0. Ah! The Old Stuff Well, before I go into an description of Anthology 1, I must say:
When I want to consider buying something, I read the "1 Star" reviews first. I want to see what people dislike about the product. I read the 1 star reviews for Anthology 1 & 2, and I found a very disturbing one about 2. It said that the Beatles were terrible, and thought they got too much credit. Well, the first thing I thought was: was the anthology the only thing you listened too? Because you can't make a good judgement if you have not listened to their actual records! Geez, how people jump right into an idea, and never come out.
The next 1 star review I read was about #1, and it complained about the poor quality of some of the songs, perticually the first couple on the 1st disk. I thought then: Well, of course it's bad quality because they made the early songs under bad recording devices! Once again the ingnorance of some people. I think ofr any Beatles fan the anthology would be a good addition to your collection. It has a lot of early stuff, not seen before. Also on the second disk includes different takes of the songs through Beatles For Sale, and live recordings.
You will also find interesting little speeches by the band, and others.

rating 1.0. BEWARE!!! Not the original songs You might think that you are getting your favorite Beatles songs here, but beware that most of them actually are rehearsal versions, different takes (some of them partial), there are several different takes of the same song here and there, and sadly, some of the most popular songs' original versions are missing (again, replaced by annoying rehearsal versions). You are MUCH better of buying separate albums than collecting this hodgepodge of "takes"
rating 5.0. Anthology 1 The Beatles Great album has several songs I have never heard before including the new song by John Lennon 'Free as a bird' I would recommend this album to all Beatle fans!!!

rating 5.0. Fun for any beatle fan It was awesome when they started this album with the new song Free as a Bird. It is a great song that really grabs you. the full alum is fun to listen to our favorite lads growing and evolving. if you get this you must get vols. 2 and 3 also just because. Highly recommend.

rating 5.0. Beatles Revisited If you love the Beatles and want to listen to some of their lesser-known tunes, this is the album for you.  It starts with my favorite, "Free as a Bird".  You will hear speeches by John Lennon, Brian Epstein, and Paul McCartney.  Did you know that they recorded "Ain't She Sweet" and "The Sheik of Araby"?  This album is full of surprises.  I would have liked to have known all the song titles before ordering this album, but surprises can be a lot of fun.  This is a 2-disc album with 60 tunes for your enjoyment.  I highly recommend this one.

rating 5.0. Anthology 1-The Beatles The Beatles from their beginnings to around the "Hard Day's Night" period.  It is great to hear some of their early, rare recordings.  Their musical skills are evident from the start and this collection demonstrates their growth as song writers.  That "skiffle" beat is nearly always present, if you listen closely, and what makes the music so infectious.  "Free As A Bird" is the first song in the Anthology set and a powerful way of opening the series.

rating 5.0. Gift This item was purchased as a gift for someone who likes the Beatles so other than shipping condition and timeliness, which were both excellent, I do not know. What i do know convinces me to buy from this company again.

rating 5.0. Revisting the Beatles Return to the Beatles . I say this only because it means it is not an alblum but out takes in the studio . It show you haw talented John Lennon and Paul McCartney were as wing songs and if you like to revisit history of Rock and Roll when the Beatles were
at thier top . You have to buy all three to see how they grew up as young lads from the night clubs in Germany to American soil . Its fun to listen to john as he fiddle with the notes to a song. I am note a good writer but I know quality when I see it. I grew up with the Beatles, my self and my cousin got an album every year for the holidays. I do not know what happen to them ,But I used to to know by memory each alblum including the famous White the album I suggest the second one each is a two CD with a story booklet
If you a Beatles fan trust me you will like these Therese addition to your collections
Bob Geco

rating 5.0. Some great early music! I purchased the other two anthologies when they first were released, but I'm not sure why this one slipped by. Some of the first tracks are crude tapings and very scratchy sounding, but history is history, which makes these cuts invaluable. It is both fun and interesting to hear the evolution of the band, from their very pop-oriented beginnings through their experimental phases. Interesting how they pretty much returned to their roots, while in turn putting a bit of an edge on those roots. Great CD set; even better anthology set!

rating 5.0. Great! This set, as do Anthology 2 and 3, give insight into the production process, the different takes gone through to create the finished product, the joking and fun between the boys in the room.  A must for all beatlemaniacs!

rating 4.0. Odds & Sods from the Fab 4 A nice collection of early Beatles recordings & outtakes. current_product_reviews: rating 5.0. Studio, Quiet Please! Of course there are three sets to this Anthology, this being the first you get to hear them creating their big hits from the beginning. Having an insight into one of the premier groups that changed music forever is really incredible. You hear John and Paul go at it back and forth over how the songs should sound and when everybody would put their part into the music. John complaining that the piano wasn't loud enough, Paul commenting on the vocals, and by the time this studio cut was made they had been playing their hits over and over. Displaying the casualness for some of the hits, and then working on new material and being picky about getting it just right.  You should enjoy this CD, it's like an "unplugged" format as they work through new material.
other_product_reviews: rating 3.0. Nice Calm Evening. I have some old Iron Butterfly, and hadn't listened to thier one hit that ran for 17 minutes in a long time. They had made a few more albums but I lost interest.  I saw this album by Mike Panera, I couldn't remember how much of a part he played in Iron Butterfly. 'In the Garden of Eden' is a mix of '80s tunes, some nice love ballads "Touch My Heart" and "In the Garden of Eden" are a couple of good love songs. "Fantasy of Love' is kind of jazzy, but he keeps interest with "Ride Captain, Ride" and "In s Gadda de Vida". Over all it's a nice mellow '80s genre album.

current_product_reviews:
rating 4.0. Early Beatles Volume 1 of the anthology series highlights many of the tunes the group recorded that were never released on disc, tunes that other artists, like the Coasters, Buddy Holly, and others performed on disc. The group's interpretations are sometimes stunning and entertaining, sometimes down right great (like "How Do You Do It"), and sometimes very raw in their performance. But it's great to see how the group evolved over the months and years early on.

These tunes are followed by alternate and studio demo/takes from their very earliest albums, plus from "A Hard Day's Night".

Like Volume #2, this disc begins with a solo, at home made, John Lennon tune, "Free As a Bird", which the other three took, added more instrumentation and vocals to it, and came up with a groovy tune.
other_product_reviews:
rating 5.0. The Beatles are a hit! This 2nd volume of the anthology features many of the alternate takes and non-released versions of many of their hits that are found on "Rubber Soul", "Sgt. Pepper" and other albums. I particularly like these because one can see how the eventual hits developed, often slowly, take after take. Some of them, like "Across the Universe", I actually prefer to the released versions.

When the anthology came out, the first two included songs done by John Lennon solo at his piano, and this particular disc features "Real Love"......in which Paul, Ringo and George add more instrumentation and vocals, giving the appearance that the group actually recorded the tune together.

rating 5.0. Bresh Rocks! I first saw Thom Bresh years ago as a virtual youngster, performing on TV, doing imitations like Jim Nabors, Andy Griffith, Willie Nelson and others, never realizing he was the son of my guitar hero, Merle Travis. Years later, I saw him perform on TV with his Dad and the two of them were pure magic. Thom has many great cuts on this album, including the tribute song that he wrote about his father, "Guitar in the Window." This live in concert version of the song is much better than the standard release, as Thom adds some "Travis" pickin' toward the end, including Merle's "I'll See You in My Dreams."
I met Thom once in Nashville, after calling his home and inquiring as to the delivery of his new CD with Buster Jones, and he personally delivered it to me at my hotel! What a class act and truly great guy......his guitar pickin' is almost identical to his Dad's, but Thom adds his own licks, making it truly a masterwork. Highly recommended for all thumb-pickers out there.

I read the 1 star reviews for Anthology 1 & 2, and I found a very disturbing one about 2. It said that the Beatles were terrible, and thought they got too much credit. Well, the first thing I thought was: was the anthology the only thing you listened too? Because you can't make a good judgement if you have not listened to their actual records! Geez, how people jump right into an idea, and never come out.
The next 1 star review I read was about #1, and it complained about the poor quality of some of the songs, perticually the first couple on the 1st disk. I thought then: Well, of course it's bad quality because they made the early songs under bad recording devices! Once again the ingnorance of some people. I think ofr any Beatles fan the anthology would be a good addition to your collection. It has a lot of early stuff, not seen before. Also on the second disk includes different takes of the songs through Beatles For Sale, and live recordings.
You will also find interesting little speeches by the band, and others.

rating 1.0. BEWARE!!! Not the original songs You might think that you are getting your favorite Beatles songs here, but beware that most of them actually are rehearsal versions, different takes (some of them partial), there are several different takes of the same song here and there, and sadly, some of the most popular songs' original versions are missing (again, replaced by annoying rehearsal versions). You are MUCH better of buying separate albums than collecting this hodgepodge of "takes"

I highly recommend this one.

"Free As A Bird" is the first song in the Anthology set and a powerful way of opening the series.

rating 5.0. Some great early music! I purchased the other two anthologies when they first were released, but I'm not sure why this one slipped by. Some of the first tracks are crude tapings and very scratchy sounding, but history is history, which makes these cuts invaluable. It is both fun and interesting to hear the evolution of the band, from their very pop-oriented beginnings through their experimental phases. Interesting how they pretty much returned to their roots, while in turn putting a bit of an edge on those roots. Great CD set; even better anthology set!

rating 5.0. Studio, Quiet Please! Of course there are three sets to this Anthology, this being the first you get to hear them creating their big hits from the beginning. Having an insight into one of the premier groups that changed music forever is really incredible. You hear John and Paul go at it back and forth over how the songs should sound and when everybody would put their part into the music. John complaining that the piano wasn't loud enough, Paul commenting on the vocals, and by the time this studio cut was made they had been playing their hits over and over. Displaying the casualness for some of the hits, and then working on new material and being picky about getting it just right.  You should enjoy this CD, it's like an "unplugged" format as they work through new material.

rating 4.0. Early Beatles Volume 1 of the anthology series highlights many of the tunes the group recorded that were never released on disc, tunes that other artists, like the Coasters, Buddy Holly, and others performed on disc. The group's interpretations are sometimes stunning and entertaining, sometimes down right great (like "How Do You Do It"), and sometimes very raw in their performance. But it's great to see how the group evolved over the months and years early on.

These tunes are followed by alternate and studio demo/takes from their very earliest albums, plus from "A Hard Day's Night".

Like Volume #2, this disc begins with a solo, at home made, John Lennon tune, "Free As a Bird", which the other three took, added more instrumentation and vocals to it, and came up with a groovy tune.

rating 5.0. The Other (early) Beatles John Lennon stated (after the Beatles broke up) that they "sold out" and turned into everything they hated. From this set we see what he meant, even if we don't agree with him! This is the "other side" of the Beatles, the raw band behind the commercial, and slightly polished facade. Before the the mass marketed hysteria of Beatlemania there was a raw and talented bunch of lads who, in the period 1957-1964, sowed the seeds of that mania that followed. This is the Beatles who were untouchable as a live act in the UK in 1963. This is the band that would send the world into a frenzy, before they actually had!

What comes through on this collection is the band's creativity and humour, and boundless confidence. Also evident is that the band was more rockabilly than their commercial product showed. We also witness how Lennon and McCartney "switched on" as songwriters.

As with the other Anthology sets, this is a priceless archive of the greatest band ever - this one in their formative years.

rating 5.0. You probably need to hear this. The Beatles were a rock group that made it very big. They were pretty ordinary when they started out, but they worked long and hard, learning all the while, fought the odds and stayed together long enough to become truly great. Because the public usually only gets to hear the finished, polished products, you might come to believe that it didn't involve that much effort - but it did, and here's some of that effort captured as it was expended. You hear the false starts, the strain on young vocal cords, the frustration, and somehow, through it all, the inspiration and final, excellent execution. Because teenage girls drowned them out with screaming, and because they initially wore silly haircuts, it's easy to assume that they were just another fad. But, you would be wrong.

rating 2.0. I hate to do this Well, I hate to do this, but I'm disappointed. When you think you're getting the complete Beatles Anthology (6 discs), something that would make your Christmas complete, and you only end up getting 1/3 of what you think you ordered, it's a let down; even if it is The Beatles.

This is what I thought I ordered:
Condition: Used - Like New - 6 CD SET ANTHOLOGY 1, 2 AND 3 WITH SLIPCASE MOTHERMACS SHIPS FROM TORONTO, ONTARIO AND BRINGS OVER 10 YEARS OF QUALITY, PROMPT SERVICE AND VALUE TO THE INTERNET.

Yay! Here, take my $$. But!

This is what I got:
A new, sealed Beatles Anthology 1 - no #2, no #3. That's 2 discs instead of 6 - bummer! And it was those discs that I was most looking forward to.

The only reason I gave this review 2 stars is because it's The Beatles, and well, "you know that can't be bad."

rating 5.0. A Must-Have for Beatles Collectors Of the three Beatles Anthology volumes, this first one is easily the most exciting for Beatles fans and collectors. The idea here is to give a new spin to the old classics, and present alternate takes, working versions, live renditions, all of which are being released here for the first time.

This volume takes fans back to the very beginning, to 1958's "That'll Be The Day." What a thrill. Then we hear home demos that were recorded in Paul's garage. This is followed by the material recorded in Hamburg with Tony Sheridan. We are treated to five selections from the Beatles' Decca Audition. Even if it wasn't the greatest tape the Beatles could've made, I'm sure Decca is stil kicking themselves now for not signing the Beatles!

It gets better and better as time goes by. The Beatles sang "I'll Get You" at the London Palladium in 1963, and "All My Loving" on Ed Sullivan in 1964. Both of those recordings are presented here. Other highlights include:

*One After 909 - I actually prefer this version to the one that got released in 1970.

*Eight Days a Week - the tight harmony at the beginning works well. Also the false starts were interesting.

*How Do You Do It - the unreleased single.

*Free As A Bird - a new Beatles masterpiece.

Die-hard Beatles fans will not be disappointed with this set!

rating 4.0. Great collection of early recordings... interesting, but rough performances. Early recording are an great insight into the formative years of the young group. I get a feeling that many recordings here are more historically important than present excellent musicianship or show the creativity that would later emerge. Yet, he rock'n'roll hits are enduring and it's enjoyable to hear them in the clubs setting. I'd hesitantly say Anthology 1 is for Beatles purists only, despite the broad appeal of the music itself. Casual Beatles fans might prefer the "Live at the BBC" recordings of the rock'n'roll hits by the Fab 4.

current_product_reviews: rating 5.0. Great compilation I was never into this band, but over the years, I'd heard a few of their songs I really enjoyed (especially "She Will Be Loved" in "The Sopranos.")  I figured I would probably find some songs I liked if I bought a compilation, but, amazingly, they actually haven't put one out yet.  I was dissapointed, but then I stumbled upon this, and it looked like just what I had wanted.  I was a bit nervous, as it was an import/unofficial release, but the disc quelled all those fears.  It sounds great, and has a great selection encompassing the band's entire career.  If you're like me, and want a compilation of this band's biggest hits, I can't recommend this strongly enough!  As an advisory - because it's an import, there's no "parental advisory" tag, and it has some big time swearing.  Not recommended as a gift for children (lots of other reviewers seem to have bought it for that purpose).

rating 4.0. Still love the music, but found a downside to having the CD I was so excited to buy this for my boys, since I was a big fan of the music as a kid.
Still fantastic music. The downside to the CD, however, is that the talking is still included; meaning for my two-yr-olds, they lose interest before the music actually comes on.
I'm sure as they get older they'll appreciate listening to the dialogue, but I wish it had been recorded as dialogue separate from the songs themselves, so that younger kids could easily fast forward to just hear the music.

rating 5.0. Enhanced rather than remixed Just received my copy of the New Recordings yesterday and, over all, I am very impressed! A great job was done enhancing these recordings! Notice I said "enhanced" rather than "remixed." Instead of completely redoing a song, only an instrument or two is added and, in some cases, it makes a world of difference. Mostly additional guitars, a saxophone, or slight orchestration is added. The extra guitars give some songs a more country feel, while the sax gives other tunes a more "blues" style. I do have one complaint. On "Fame and Fortune," there appears to be this strange static sound that is heard on the new sax accompaniment. I first thought it was in my speakers, but it was in the cd. I wonder if that sound is intentional or that it could not be helped due to the remastering? It's a little annoying, but nothing major. I love the fact that the temptation to do a "greatest hits" package (we get enough of them from RCA/Sony) was avoided. I've always loved the soft ballads that Elvis recorded in the early sixties and this cd has a lot of my favorite songs. The original masters were used and respected, unlike the "Viva Elvis" cd that was, in my opinion, overkill. I especially enjoy how some of the songs were extended making their running time longer. Purist may not like the fake duet on "Love Me Tender," but I think it sounds much better than the duet on the "Viva Elvis" cd. I think even die hard purist will find something to like on this cd. This would be a great way to introduce Elvis to the younger generation. Great cd again! Hope there will be a vol 2!!!

rating 1.0. This CD goes against the wishes of Elvis. The producer's comments on the reviews ruin what little enjoyment the CD gives There is a blurb on all the websites advertising this CD that finally they have complied with Elvis's wishes and brought his vocals out in the forefront. However, this is completely wrong. The producer, Larry Jordan, will probably start arguing about this with me now (EDIT--just as I predicted, he has) in the comments to my review. (EDIT--now he has started asking Amazon to delete any and all comments on the reviews that make the CD look bad, see below. He is also personally attacking the taste and credibility of every single person who has posted a negative review on this site, in the review comments). If you read Guralnick's acclaimed Elvis biographies, you will see that Elvis complained repeatedly that Col. Parker tampered with his recordings to bring his voice out more. So, on the original albums Elvis thought his voice was too loud and he wanted to hear the backup singers more. On the New Recordings, Elvis would not be happy that his voice is now three times as loud as he thought it should be and the backup singers are even quieter.

The main thing that is wrong with this CD is that the versions are too close to the originals. I had no problem with "Viva Elvis," for instance, because they were doing different things with the songs. Here, they add just a few instruments here and there in the empty spaces, which ends up in most cases making the songs feel just a bit too cluttered. When you go back and hear the original albums, you begin to "hear" the New Recordings versions in your mind and ask "where is that corny saxophone? Isn't it supposed to play here?"

There are a handful of arrangements that ruined some of the songs that just should not have been tampered with. I am no purist. If something is different and it works, I like it. The worst part of this CD is the jazz saxophones that are just "doing their thing" all over the songs, sometimes even while Elvis is singing. It's a bit too much and it's like, "will you please stop?" Also, I thought the strings were fake, but I was wrong. There are actual string musicians. The string arrangements sometimes seem intrusive or badly placed.

(EDIT) Here is a more detailed breakdown of which songs don't work for me:

First, the "sax offenders":

So Glad You're Mine: The sax on this song is very out of tune (flat sounding) and annoying.
Stuck On You: An example of that busy sax just "doing it all over it."
I Gotta Know: Busy sax doing it even more and even worse this time.
Soldier Boy: More of the same..another busy sax doing its annoying thing all over the song.
Fame and Fortune: Not quite as bad as the others, but this "smooth jazz" sax sound is annoying.

And on to the rest:

Love Me Tender: A bad country duet with a woman.
Good Luck Charm: The guitar doesn't quite work.
Home Is Where The Heart Is: The flute doesn't belong here.
Can't Help Falling In Love: The string arrangement is cliche.
Anything That's Part of You: More cliche strings.
In My Way: The strings here are too busy and ruin the intimacy of the song.

I like the album even less after seeing all of the remarks from the album's producer and friends on all of these Amazon reviews. There is definitely a "campaign" at work here.

rating 5.0. ALWAYS ELVIS I am listening to this cd for the sixth time as I write this.Elvis would be very proud of this one,the change of instruments,removal of some of the overpowering background vocals,and his voice out front and center makes this a cd that you can play over and over,while driving,working,or carrying on a conversation with someone.The song selection is great and brings back many fond memories.It is like hearing these songs for the first time and clearly demonstrates the greatness and perfection of the voice of Elvis Presley.If you want a really enjoyable Elvis cd,this would be the one.The mix of songs flow so well.I just can't say enough good things about this cd,just buy it and enjoy.It will bring to memory that Elvis was and is the greatest.

rating 5.0. A Grand Slam Amongst Single Hitters Even a purist as myself has fallen in love with this new cd. I never thought in my life that I would wish to hear Elvis's music any other way than how I have been for the past 40 years. But here it is! That breath of fresh air! Why no one had thought of releasing a cd of Elvis before in this manner is beyond me. Elvis has been given a new lease on life. Not that he really needed one. But we needed it! Twenty Six Elvis songs played as you have never heard them before. Each song lightly accented with either strings, a sax, new backing vocals and even bongos on a song such as "Fever". This producer knew not to over saturate each and every song with way too much new instrumentation. Just enough is added that is very enjoyable but never compromises the legend we know as Elvis. "I Can't Help Falling In Love" has never sounded better than it does on this disc.  With an added Nashville string arrangement, there is no doubt in my mind that if Elvis could go back and add it, he would. It is that beautiful. "Love Me Tender" is another one that has never sounded better. On this disc, Elvis is partnered with the sexy voice of Cassie West for a duet that is better and makes more sense that any of those Christmas duets released a few years ago. Another one that I don't believe has ever been released on a cd is "Home Is Where The Heart Is' which was featured on Elvis' Kid Galahad EP. With an added flute and string overlay, this is the one gem for those in search of this record's grail. Absolutely beautiful. "Reconsider Baby" is the track on this record where nothing has been added except for a remastering that this song has needed for 54 years. The fact that no new instrumentation was added, shows that this producer knew when to add or not add new instrumentation. A year ago I would have been a against something like this. But now that I have heard it, I realized that this was what was needed. A purist like myself needed to come out of the middle ages. I wish RCA/BMG/Sony would also. My Boy! My Boy!
rating 3.0. King in great voice, but arrangements not,,, Love hearing his voice in a different setting, but the new arrangements are not very good or complementary.

rating 3.0. 5 Stars For The Technology, 2 For The Song Selection (Read "Comments" Section) With the very honourable exception of "Reconsider, Baby", long recognized by discriminating Elvis fans as among his very greatest recordings, this is a pretty lame song selection. Yes, Elvis is in fine vocal form throughout, but most of the songs are second rate. Even such top hits as "Stuck On You", "She's Not You" and "Good Luck Charm" are hardly first rate Presley material, though they are better listening experiences here than they were originally. With so many great recordings of better material available, such as "Any Day Now" and "Stranger In My Own Home Town", to name just 2 of the songs from the legendary 1969 American Studios recordings brilliantly sung but seriously compromised by overdubs of voice, horns and strings begging for a new treatment worthy of the performances on the undoctored masters, why choose all these so so ballads? That said, and with the exception of some overly busy added tenor sax playing at times, this is a very interesting approach. Here's hoping the producer will extend his efforts into a more tasteful selection of material and really give us something revelatory.

rating 5.0. 5 Stars This CD is one of the best "do over" Elvis music I have heard.  I really enjoy the songs (most of them are favorites of mine) and like the change in the background instruments.  The only one I'm not real crazy about is the duet Love Me Tender.  It's okay but just not to my liking.  I have been an Elvis fan since  the movie "Love Me Tender" and will continue to do so for as long as I live.  Thank you for releasing this CD.

rating 5.0. on time and packaged nicely. It is a must for anyone who loves ... This cd arrived as promised, on time and packaged nicely.  It is a must for anyone who loves Elvis music. It is worth it to read the insert and then listen for the changes.  Wonderful!

rating 5.0. Subtle changes, Nice listen. Very nicely done. The added instruments and lowering of the background environment that sometimes took away from the main attraction, is done in a subtle yet noticeable way here. Though I am one that has been an Elvis fan since birth, (it seems), and I usually don't like re-mixes of his music, this collection of songs was done subtly enough that makes it a good listen. A little change, but the avid Elvis fan will hear the difference right away, whereas the casual Elvis fan may not even notice it's been changed, unless playing the original version and this one back to back.

Overall, it's a good selection of songs, a little different then the usual "most popular" song lists we get from RCA or Sony.

rating 5.0. Buy This Album Elvis Fans - It's Your Duty! Excellent CD! The addition of strings, electric and slide guitars, sax, clarinet, flute, percussion, organ, etc on the various songs made long-time classics even more vibrant for today. After five decades of being an Elvis fan, to have his music generate new emotions is a tribute to the producer, musicians and technicians who worked on this collection.  "Fame and Fortune" brought a big smile; "Can't Help Falling In Love" brought teary eyes and "Fever" made me feel like I was sitting in Ricky Ricardo's smoke filled Tropicana Club!  More jazzy and bluesy, softening of background vocals at times when appropriate while at other times the background vocals were left where they should be.  Beautiful substitution of new instruments where background vocals were softened.  Nicely blended and augmented new instrumentation along with original instrumentation. If producers of Elvis' music would follow this pattern of adding new instrumentation to augment original recordings and balance the background vocals, I would be buying Elvis CD's like I was a teenager again.  Great Job!

rating 4.0. not great... very enjoyable and worth the money very clean reporting. nice to hear Elvis is voice so pure and loud. wish with a record of the new music it would have been edgier and more modern. the music sticks to the classic sound overall very good CD.

rating 4.0. I enjoyed most of what took place with Elvis' music I enjoyed most of what took place with Elvis' music.  I did really like how his voice was more up front but a little disappointed on some of the backing harmonies that were way back in the mix and either disappeared.  Adding some additional instruments was fun to listen to on most songs.  The song She's Not You went from a pop song to country.  Fever still had that bluesy sound but felt like I was hearing it in a dingy lit night club.  The song Suck On You didn't work for me.  Some songs when adding an extra instrument (sax on this one) all of a sudden sounded like to many notes where playing and getting in the way. rating 5.0. Enhanced rather than remixed Just received my copy of the New Recordings yesterday and, over all, I am very impressed! A great job was done enhancing these recordings! Notice I said "enhanced" rather than "remixed." Instead of completely redoing a song, only an instrument or two is added and, in some cases, it makes a world of difference. Mostly additional guitars, a saxophone, or slight orchestration is added. The extra guitars give some songs a more country feel, while the sax gives other tunes a more "blues" style. I do have one complaint. On "Fame and Fortune," there appears to be this strange static sound that is heard on the new sax accompaniment. I first thought it was in my speakers, but it was in the cd. I wonder if that sound is intentional or that it could not be helped due to the remastering? It's a little annoying, but nothing major. I love the fact that the temptation to do a "greatest hits" package (we get enough of them from RCA/Sony) was avoided. I've always loved the soft ballads that Elvis recorded in the early sixties and this cd has a lot of my favorite songs. The original masters were used and respected, unlike the "Viva Elvis" cd that was, in my opinion, overkill. I especially enjoy how some of the songs were extended making their running time longer. Purist may not like the fake duet on "Love Me Tender," but I think it sounds much better than the duet on the "Viva Elvis" cd. I think even die hard purist will find something to like on this cd. This would be a great way to introduce Elvis to the younger generation. Great cd again! Hope there will be a vol 2!!!

rating 1.0. This CD goes against the wishes of Elvis. The producer's comments on the reviews ruin what little enjoyment the CD gives There is a blurb on all the websites advertising this CD that finally they have complied with Elvis's wishes and brought his vocals out in the forefront. However, this is completely wrong. The producer, Larry Jordan, will probably start arguing about this with me now (EDIT--just as I predicted, he has) in the comments to my review. (EDIT--now he has started asking Amazon to delete any and all comments on the reviews that make the CD look bad, see below. He is also personally attacking the taste and credibility of every single person who has posted a negative review on this site, in the review comments). If you read Guralnick's acclaimed Elvis biographies, you will see that Elvis complained repeatedly that Col. Parker tampered with his recordings to bring his voice out more. So, on the original albums Elvis thought his voice was too loud and he wanted to hear the backup singers more. On the New Recordings, Elvis would not be happy that his voice is now three times as loud as he thought it should be and the backup singers are even quieter.

The main thing that is wrong with this CD is that the versions are too close to the originals. I had no problem with "Viva Elvis," for instance, because they were doing different things with the songs. Here, they add just a few instruments here and there in the empty spaces, which ends up in most cases making the songs feel just a bit too cluttered. When you go back and hear the original albums, you begin to "hear" the New Recordings versions in your mind and ask "where is that corny saxophone? Isn't it supposed to play here?"

There are a handful of arrangements that ruined some of the songs that just should not have been tampered with. I am no purist. If something is different and it works, I like it. The worst part of this CD is the jazz saxophones that are just "doing their thing" all over the songs, sometimes even while Elvis is singing. It's a bit too much and it's like, "will you please stop?" Also, I thought the strings were fake, but I was wrong. There are actual string musicians. The string arrangements sometimes seem intrusive or badly placed.

(EDIT) Here is a more detailed breakdown of which songs don't work for me:

First, the "sax offenders":

So Glad You're Mine: The sax on this song is very out of tune (flat sounding) and annoying.
Stuck On You: An example of that busy sax just "doing it all over it."
I Gotta Know: Busy sax doing it even more and even worse this time.
Soldier Boy: More of the same..another busy sax doing its annoying thing all over the song.
Fame and Fortune: Not quite as bad as the others, but this "smooth jazz" sax sound is annoying.

And on to the rest:

Love Me Tender: A bad country duet with a woman.
Good Luck Charm: The guitar doesn't quite work.
Home Is Where The Heart Is: The flute doesn't belong here.
Can't Help Falling In Love: The string arrangement is cliche.
Anything That's Part of You: More cliche strings.
In My Way: The strings here are too busy and ruin the intimacy of the song.

I like the album even less after seeing all of the remarks from the album's producer and friends on all of these Amazon reviews. There is definitely a "campaign" at work here.

rating 5.0. ALWAYS ELVIS I am listening to this cd for the sixth time as I write this.Elvis would be very proud of this one,the change of instruments,removal of some of the overpowering background vocals,and his voice out front and center makes this a cd that you can play over and over,while driving,working,or carrying on a conversation with someone.The song selection is great and brings back many fond memories.It is like hearing these songs for the first time and clearly demonstrates the greatness and perfection of the voice of Elvis Presley.If you want a really enjoyable Elvis cd,this would be the one.The mix of songs flow so well.I just can't say enough good things about this cd,just buy it and enjoy.It will bring to memory that Elvis was and is the greatest.

rating 5.0. A Grand Slam Amongst Single Hitters Even a purist as myself has fallen in love with this new cd. I never thought in my life that I would wish to hear Elvis's music any other way than how I have been for the past 40 years. But here it is! That breath of fresh air! Why no one had thought of releasing a cd of Elvis before in this manner is beyond me. Elvis has been given a new lease on life. Not that he really needed one. But we needed it! Twenty Six Elvis songs played as you have never heard them before. Each song lightly accented with either strings, a sax, new backing vocals and even bongos on a song such as "Fever". This producer knew not to over saturate each and every song with way too much new instrumentation. Just enough is added that is very enjoyable but never compromises the legend we know as Elvis. "I Can't Help Falling In Love" has never sounded better than it does on this disc.  With an added Nashville string arrangement, there is no doubt in my mind that if Elvis could go back and add it, he would. It is that beautiful. "Love Me Tender" is another one that has never sounded better. On this disc, Elvis is partnered with the sexy voice of Cassie West for a duet that is better and makes more sense that any of those Christmas duets released a few years ago. Another one that I don't believe has ever been released on a cd is "Home Is Where The Heart Is' which was featured on Elvis' Kid Galahad EP. With an added flute and string overlay, this is the one gem for those in search of this record's grail. Absolutely beautiful. "Reconsider Baby" is the track on this record where nothing has been added except for a remastering that this song has needed for 54 years. The fact that no new instrumentation was added, shows that this producer knew when to add or not add new instrumentation. A year ago I would have been a against something like this. But now that I have heard it, I realized that this was what was needed. A purist like myself needed to come out of the middle ages. I wish RCA/BMG/Sony would also. My Boy! My Boy!
rating 3.0. King in great voice, but arrangements not,,, Love hearing his voice in a different setting, but the new arrangements are not very good or complementary.

rating 3.0. 5 Stars For The Technology, 2 For The Song Selection (Read "Comments" Section) With the very honourable exception of "Reconsider, Baby", long recognized by discriminating Elvis fans as among his very greatest recordings, this is a pretty lame song selection. Yes, Elvis is in fine vocal form throughout, but most of the songs are second rate. Even such top hits as "Stuck On You", "She's Not You" and "Good Luck Charm" are hardly first rate Presley material, though they are better listening experiences here than they were originally. With so many great recordings of better material available, such as "Any Day Now" and "Stranger In My Own Home Town", to name just 2 of the songs from the legendary 1969 American Studios recordings brilliantly sung but seriously compromised by overdubs of voice, horns and strings begging for a new treatment worthy of the performances on the undoctored masters, why choose all these so so ballads? That said, and with the exception of some overly busy added tenor sax playing at times, this is a very interesting approach. Here's hoping the producer will extend his efforts into a more tasteful selection of material and really give us something revelatory.

rating 5.0. 5 Stars This CD is one of the best "do over" Elvis music I have heard.  I really enjoy the songs (most of them are favorites of mine) and like the change in the background instruments.  The only one I'm not real crazy about is the duet Love Me Tender.  It's okay but just not to my liking.  I have been an Elvis fan since  the movie "Love Me Tender" and will continue to do so for as long as I live.  Thank you for releasing this CD.

rating 5.0. on time and packaged nicely. It is a must for anyone who loves ... This cd arrived as promised, on time and packaged nicely.  It is a must for anyone who loves Elvis music. It is worth it to read the insert and then listen for the changes.  Wonderful!

rating 5.0. Subtle changes, Nice listen. Very nicely done. The added instruments and lowering of the background environment that sometimes took away from the main attraction, is done in a subtle yet noticeable way here. Though I am one that has been an Elvis fan since birth, (it seems), and I usually don't like re-mixes of his music, this collection of songs was done subtly enough that makes it a good listen. A little change, but the avid Elvis fan will hear the difference right away, whereas the casual Elvis fan may not even notice it's been changed, unless playing the original version and this one back to back.

Overall, it's a good selection of songs, a little different then the usual "most popular" song lists we get from RCA or Sony.

rating 5.0. Buy This Album Elvis Fans - It's Your Duty! Excellent CD! The addition of strings, electric and slide guitars, sax, clarinet, flute, percussion, organ, etc on the various songs made long-time classics even more vibrant for today. After five decades of being an Elvis fan, to have his music generate new emotions is a tribute to the producer, musicians and technicians who worked on this collection.  "Fame and Fortune" brought a big smile; "Can't Help Falling In Love" brought teary eyes and "Fever" made me feel like I was sitting in Ricky Ricardo's smoke filled Tropicana Club!  More jazzy and bluesy, softening of background vocals at times when appropriate while at other times the background vocals were left where they should be.  Beautiful substitution of new instruments where background vocals were softened.  Nicely blended and augmented new instrumentation along with original instrumentation. If producers of Elvis' music would follow this pattern of adding new instrumentation to augment original recordings and balance the background vocals, I would be buying Elvis CD's like I was a teenager again.  Great Job!

I did really like how his voice was more up front but a little disappointed on some of the backing harmonies that were way back in the mix and either disappeared.  Adding some additional instruments was fun to listen to on most songs.  The song She's Not You went from a pop song to country.  Fever still had that bluesy sound but felt like I was hearing it in a dingy lit night club.  The song Suck On You didn't work for me.  Some songs when adding an extra instrument (sax on this one) all of a sudden sounded like to many notes where playing and getting in the way.  Overall it was fun to listen to and I would recommend it to Elvis fans.

rating 5.0. Really good! I enjoyed Elvis in my younger days, I must admit, and was a collector of most of his movie soundtracks as the movies came out. He's in good/great voice for these tracks and to my ear the background/support changes add to the musical experience significantly, though many of these songs I've not listened to for a whole lot of decades. Updating their presentation while keeping his voice front and center adds to the experience, and hence the title of "new recordings'.. Highly recommend! Great sampling of Elvis, the singing voice. I hope they try this with other songs of his as well. This along with "That's the Way It Is" has become my two favorite albums of Elvis music.

He is also personally attacking the taste and credibility of every single person who has posted a negative review on this site, in the review comments). If you read Guralnick's acclaimed Elvis biographies, you will see that Elvis complained repeatedly that Col. Parker tampered with his recordings to bring his voice out more. So, on the original albums Elvis thought his voice was too loud and he wanted to hear the backup singers more. On the New Recordings, Elvis would not be happy that his voice is now three times as loud as he thought it should be and the backup singers are even quieter.

The main thing that is wrong with this CD is that the versions are too close to the originals. I had no problem with "Viva Elvis," for instance, because they were doing different things with the songs. Here, they add just a few instruments here and there in the empty spaces, which ends up in most cases making the songs feel just a bit too cluttered. When you go back and hear the original albums, you begin to "hear" the New Recordings versions in your mind and ask "where is that corny saxophone? Isn't it supposed to play here?"

There are a handful of arrangements that ruined some of the songs that just should not have been tampered with. I am no purist. If something is different and it works, I like it. The worst part of this CD is the jazz saxophones that are just "doing their thing" all over the songs, sometimes even while Elvis is singing. It's a bit too much and it's like, "will you please stop?" Also, I thought the strings were fake, but I was wrong. There are actual string musicians. The string arrangements sometimes seem intrusive or badly placed.

(EDIT) Here is a more detailed breakdown of which songs don't work for me:

First, the "sax offenders":

So Glad You're Mine: The sax on this song is very out of tune (flat sounding) and annoying.
Stuck On You: An example of that busy sax just "doing it all over it."
I Gotta Know: Busy sax doing it even more and even worse this time.
Soldier Boy: More of the same..another busy sax doing its annoying thing all over the song.
Fame and Fortune: Not quite as bad as the others, but this "smooth jazz" sax sound is annoying.

And on to the rest:

Love Me Tender: A bad country duet with a woman.
Good Luck Charm: The guitar doesn't quite work.
Home Is Where The Heart Is: The flute doesn't belong here.
Can't Help Falling In Love: The string arrangement is cliche.
Anything That's Part of You: More cliche strings.
In My Way: The strings here are too busy and ruin the intimacy of the song.

I like the album even less after seeing all of the remarks from the album's producer and friends on all of these Amazon reviews. There is definitely a "campaign" at work here.

ALWAYS ELVIS I am listening to this cd for the sixth time as I write this.Elvis would be very proud of this one,the change of instruments,removal of some of the overpowering background vocals,and his voice out front and center makes this a cd that you can play over and over,while driving,working,or carrying on a conversation with someone.The song selection is great and brings back many fond memories.It is like hearing these songs for the first time and clearly demonstrates the greatness and perfection of the voice of Elvis Presley.If you want a really enjoyable Elvis cd,this would be the one.The mix of songs flow so well.I just can't say enough good things about this cd,just buy it and enjoy.It will bring to memory that Elvis was and is the greatest.

rating 5.0. A Grand Slam Amongst Single Hitters Even a purist as myself has fallen in love with this new cd. I never thought in my life that I would wish to hear Elvis's music any other way than how I have been for the past 40 years. But here it is! That breath of fresh air! Why no one had thought of releasing a cd of Elvis before in this manner is beyond me. Elvis has been given a new lease on life. Not that he really needed one. But we needed it! Twenty Six Elvis songs played as you have never heard them before. Each song lightly accented with either strings, a sax, new backing vocals and even bongos on a song such as "Fever". This producer knew not to over saturate each and every song with way too much new instrumentation. Just enough is added that is very enjoyable but never compromises the legend we know as Elvis. "I Can't Help Falling In Love" has never sounded better than it does on this disc.  With an added Nashville string arrangement, there is no doubt in my mind that if Elvis could go back and add it, he would. It is that beautiful. "Love Me Tender" is another one that has never sounded better. On this disc, Elvis is partnered with the sexy voice of Cassie West for a duet that is better and makes more sense that any of those Christmas duets released a few years ago. Another one that I don't believe has ever been released on a cd is "Home Is Where The Heart Is' which was featured on Elvis' Kid Galahad EP. With an added flute and string overlay, this is the one gem for those in search of this record's grail. Absolutely beautiful. "Reconsider Baby" is the track on this record where nothing has been added except for a remastering that this song has needed for 54 years. The fact that no new instrumentation was added, shows that this producer knew when to add or not add new instrumentation. A year ago I would have been a against something like this. But now that I have heard it, I realized that this was what was needed. A purist like myself needed to come out of the middle ages. I wish RCA/BMG/Sony would also. My Boy! My Boy!
rating 3.0. King in great voice, but arrangements not,,, Love hearing his voice in a different setting, but the new arrangements are not very good or complementary.

rating 3.0. 5 Stars For The Technology, 2 For The Song Selection (Read "Comments" Section) With the very honourable exception of "Reconsider, Baby", long recognized by discriminating Elvis fans as among his very greatest recordings, this is a pretty lame song selection. Yes, Elvis is in fine vocal form throughout, but most of the songs are second rate. Even such top hits as "Stuck On You", "She's Not You" and "Good Luck Charm" are hardly first rate Presley material, though they are better listening experiences here than they were originally. With so many great recordings of better material available, such as "Any Day Now" and "Stranger In My Own Home Town", to name just 2 of the songs from the legendary 1969 American Studios recordings brilliantly sung but seriously compromised by overdubs of voice, horns and strings begging for a new treatment worthy of the performances on the undoctored masters, why choose all these so so ballads? That said, and with the exception of some overly busy added tenor sax playing at times, this is a very interesting approach. Here's hoping the producer will extend his efforts into a more tasteful selection of material and really give us something revelatory.

rating 5.0. Subtle changes, Nice listen. Very nicely done. The added instruments and lowering of the background environment that sometimes took away from the main attraction, is done in a subtle yet noticeable way here. Though I am one that has been an Elvis fan since birth, (it seems), and I usually don't like re-mixes of his music, this collection of songs was done subtly enough that makes it a good listen. A little change, but the avid Elvis fan will hear the difference right away, whereas the casual Elvis fan may not even notice it's been changed, unless playing the original version and this one back to back.

Overall, it's a good selection of songs, a little different then the usual "most popular" song lists we get from RCA or Sony.

rating 5.0. Buy This Album Elvis Fans - It's Your Duty! Excellent CD! The addition of strings, electric and slide guitars, sax, clarinet, flute, percussion, organ, etc on the various songs made long-time classics even more vibrant for today. After five decades of being an Elvis fan, to have his music generate new emotions is a tribute to the producer, musicians and technicians who worked on this collection.  "Fame and Fortune" brought a big smile; "Can't Help Falling In Love" brought teary eyes and "Fever" made me feel like I was sitting in Ricky Ricardo's smoke filled Tropicana Club!  More jazzy and bluesy, softening of background vocals at times when appropriate while at other times the background vocals were left where they should be.  Beautiful substitution of new instruments where background vocals were softened.  Nicely blended and augmented new instrumentation along with original instrumentation. If producers of Elvis' music would follow this pattern of adding new instrumentation to augment original recordings and balance the background vocals, I would be buying Elvis CD's like I was a teenager again.  Great Job!

rating 5.0. Enhanced rather than remixed Just received my copy of the New Recordings yesterday and, over all, I am very impressed! A great job was done enhancing these recordings! Notice I said "enhanced" rather than "remixed." Instead of completely redoing a song, only an instrument or two is added and, in some cases, it makes a world of difference. Mostly additional guitars, a saxophone, or slight orchestration is added. The extra guitars give some songs a more country feel, while the sax gives other tunes a more "blues" style. I do have one complaint. On "Fame and Fortune," there appears to be this strange static sound that is heard on the new sax accompaniment. I first thought it was in my speakers, but it was in the cd. I wonder if that sound is intentional or that it could not be helped due to the remastering? It's a little annoying, but nothing major. I love the fact that the temptation to do a "greatest hits" package (we get enough of them from RCA/Sony) was avoided. I've always loved the soft ballads that Elvis recorded in the early sixties and this cd has a lot of my favorite songs. The original masters were used and respected, unlike the "Viva Elvis" cd that was, in my opinion, overkill. I especially enjoy how some of the songs were extended making their running time longer. Purist may not like the fake duet on "Love Me Tender," but I think it sounds much better than the duet on the "Viva Elvis" cd. I think even die hard purist will find something to like on this cd. This would be a great way to introduce Elvis to the younger generation. Great cd again! Hope there will be a vol 2!!!

current_product_reviews: rating 4.0. A New Interesting Take On The Familar This was a very interesting CD. While I'm used to the very modern techno productions done to Elvis' recordings this is different. Additional instrumentation here was used to enhance the recordings. For the most part, to my taste, some were slightly improved especially where strings were added, but I sometimes found the additional saxophone to be obtrusive. There were actually times where I could have done with less, not more enhancement. I realize that this is a very subjective and personal feeling since these are recordings I am very used to and very comfortable with as I have heard them in a certain way most of my life. The choice of songs was interesting as, for the most part, hits were avoided. Only 10 charted songs are here out of a total of 26 tracks and half of those are charted 'B' sides. The songs are from the 1956-62 era [in the public domain in Europe] and cover much ground musically.  Only one song was turned into a duet: "Love Me Tender" with Cassie West. In some cases the power of the Jordanaires' vocal support was diminished, and in addition to the aforementioned saxophone and strings, guitar, accordion, flute and other instruments have been added. Overall it's a mixed bag with much more positive than negative and is certainly worth the cost to listen to these recordings as you have never heard them before. The picture of an early sixties Elvis is very appropriate as only 2 songs are from the fifties.  I would like to see this production team take on another 26 of Elvis' songs and issue a Volume 2.

rating 5.0. Enhanced rather than remixed Just received my copy of the New Recordings yesterday and, over all, I am very impressed! A great job was done enhancing these recordings! Notice I said "enhanced" rather than "remixed." Instead of completely redoing a song, only an instrument or two is added and, in some cases, it makes a world of difference. Mostly additional guitars, a saxophone, or slight orchestration is added. The extra guitars give some songs a more country feel, while the sax gives other tunes a more "blues" style. I do have one complaint. On "Fame and Fortune," there appears to be this strange static sound that is heard on the new sax accompaniment. I first thought it was in my speakers, but it was in the cd. I wonder if that sound is intentional or that it could not be helped due to the remastering? It's a little annoying, but nothing major. I love the fact that the temptation to do a "greatest hits" package (we get enough of them from RCA/Sony) was avoided. I've always loved the soft ballads that Elvis recorded in the early sixties and this cd has a lot of my favorite songs. The original masters were used and respected, unlike the "Viva Elvis" cd that was, in my opinion, overkill. I especially enjoy how some of the songs were extended making their running time longer. Purist may not like the fake duet on "Love Me Tender," but I think it sounds much better than the duet on the "Viva Elvis" cd. I think even die hard purist will find something to like on this cd. This would be a great way to introduce Elvis to the younger generation. Great cd again! Hope there will be a vol 2!!!

rating 1.0. This CD goes against the wishes of Elvis. The producer's comments on the reviews ruin what little enjoyment the CD gives There is a blurb on all the websites advertising this CD that finally they have complied with Elvis's wishes and brought his vocals out in the forefront. However, this is completely wrong. The producer, Larry Jordan, will probably start arguing about this with me now (EDIT--just as I predicted, he has) in the comments to my review. (EDIT--now he has started asking Amazon to delete any and all comments on the reviews that make the CD look bad, see below. He is also personally attacking the taste and credibility of every single person who has posted a negative review on this site, in the review comments). If you read Guralnick's acclaimed Elvis biographies, you will see that Elvis complained repeatedly that Col. Parker tampered with his recordings to bring his voice out more. So, on the original albums Elvis thought his voice was too loud and he wanted to hear the backup singers more. On the New Recordings, Elvis would not be happy that his voice is now three times as loud as he thought it should be and the backup singers are even quieter.

The main thing that is wrong with this CD is that the versions are too close to the originals. I had no problem with "Viva Elvis," for instance, because they were doing different things with the songs. Here, they add just a few instruments here and there in the empty spaces, which ends up in most cases making the songs feel just a bit too cluttered. When you go back and hear the original albums, you begin to "hear" the New Recordings versions in your mind and ask "where is that corny saxophone? Isn't it supposed to play here?"

There are a handful of arrangements that ruined some of the songs that just should not have been tampered with. I am no purist. If something is different and it works, I like it. The worst part of this CD is the jazz saxophones that are just "doing their thing" all over the songs, sometimes even while Elvis is singing. It's a bit too much and it's like, "will you please stop?" Also, I thought the strings were fake, but I was wrong. There are actual string musicians. The string arrangements sometimes seem intrusive or badly placed.

(EDIT) Here is a more detailed breakdown of which songs don't work for me:

First, the "sax offenders":

So Glad You're Mine: The sax on this song is very out of tune (flat sounding) and annoying.
Stuck On You: An example of that busy sax just "doing it all over it."
I Gotta Know: Busy sax doing it even more and even worse this time.
Soldier Boy: More of the same..another busy sax doing its annoying thing all over the song.
Fame and Fortune: Not quite as bad as the others, but this "smooth jazz" sax sound is annoying.

And on to the rest:

Love Me Tender: A bad country duet with a woman.
Good Luck Charm: The guitar doesn't quite work.
Home Is Where The Heart Is: The flute doesn't belong here.
Can't Help Falling In Love: The string arrangement is cliche.
Anything That's Part of You: More cliche strings.
In My Way: The strings here are too busy and ruin the intimacy of the song.

I like the album even less after seeing all of the remarks from the album's producer and friends on all of these Amazon reviews. There is definitely a "campaign" at work here.

ALWAYS ELVIS I am listening to this cd for the sixth time as I write this.Elvis would be very proud of this one,the change of instruments,removal of some of the overpowering background vocals,and his voice out front and center makes this a cd that you can play over and over,while driving,working,or carrying on a conversation with someone.The song selection is great and brings back many fond memories.It is like hearing these songs for the first time and clearly demonstrates the greatness and perfection of the voice of Elvis Presley.If you want a really enjoyable Elvis cd,this would be the one.The mix of songs flow so well.I just can't say enough good things about this cd,just buy it and enjoy.It will bring to memory that Elvis was and is the greatest.

rating 5.0. A Grand Slam Amongst Single Hitters Even a purist as myself has fallen in love with this new cd. I never thought in my life that I would wish to hear Elvis's music any other way than how I have been for the past 40 years. But here it is! That breath of fresh air! Why no one had thought of releasing a cd of Elvis before in this manner is beyond me. Elvis has been given a new lease on life. Not that he really needed one. But we needed it! Twenty Six Elvis songs played as you have never heard them before. Each song lightly accented with either strings, a sax, new backing vocals and even bongos on a song such as "Fever". This producer knew not to over saturate each and every song with way too much new instrumentation. Just enough is added that is very enjoyable but never compromises the legend we know as Elvis. "I Can't Help Falling In Love" has never sounded better than it does on this disc.  With an added Nashville string arrangement, there is no doubt in my mind that if Elvis could go back and add it, he would. It is that beautiful. "Love Me Tender" is another one that has never sounded better. On this disc, Elvis is partnered with the sexy voice of Cassie West for a duet that is better and makes more sense that any of those Christmas duets released a few years ago. Another one that I don't believe has ever been released on a cd is "Home Is Where The Heart Is' which was featured on Elvis' Kid Galahad EP. With an added flute and string overlay, this is the one gem for those in search of this record's grail. Absolutely beautiful. "Reconsider Baby" is the track on this record where nothing has been added except for a remastering that this song has needed for 54 years. The fact that no new instrumentation was added, shows that this producer knew when to add or not add new instrumentation. A year ago I would have been a against something like this. But now that I have heard it, I realized that this was what was needed. A purist like myself needed to come out of the middle ages. I wish RCA/BMG/Sony would also. My Boy! My Boy!
rating 3.0. King in great voice, but arrangements not,,, Love hearing his voice in a different setting, but the new arrangements are not very good or complementary.

rating 3.0. 5 Stars For The Technology, 2 For The Song Selection (Read "Comments" Section) With the very honourable exception of "Reconsider, Baby", long recognized by discriminating Elvis fans as among his very greatest recordings, this is a pretty lame song selection. Yes, Elvis is in fine vocal form throughout, but most of the songs are second rate. Even such top hits as "Stuck On You", "She's Not You" and "Good Luck Charm" are hardly first rate Presley material, though they are better listening experiences here than they were originally. With so many great recordings of better material available, such as "Any Day Now" and "Stranger In My Own Home Town", to name just 2 of the songs from the legendary 1969 American Studios recordings brilliantly sung but seriously compromised by overdubs of voice, horns and strings begging for a new treatment worthy of the performances on the undoctored masters, why choose all these so so ballads? That said, and with the exception of some overly busy added tenor sax playing at times, this is a very interesting approach. Here's hoping the producer will extend his efforts into a more tasteful selection of material and really give us something revelatory.

I have been an Elvis fan since  the movie "Love Me Tender" and will continue to do so for as long as I live.  Thank you for releasing this CD.
