rating 5.0. Subtle changes, Nice listen. Very nicely done. The added instruments and lowering of the background environment that sometimes took away from the main attraction, is done in a subtle yet noticeable way here. Though I am one that has been an Elvis fan since birth, (it seems), and I usually don't like re-mixes of his music, this collection of songs was done subtly enough that makes it a good listen. A little change, but the avid Elvis fan will hear the difference right away, whereas the casual Elvis fan may not even notice it's been changed, unless playing the original version and this one back to back.

Overall, it's a good selection of songs, a little different then the usual "most popular" song lists we get from RCA or Sony.

rating 5.0. Buy This Album Elvis Fans - It's Your Duty! Excellent CD! The addition of strings, electric and slide guitars, sax, clarinet, flute, percussion, organ, etc on the various songs made long-time classics even more vibrant for today. After five decades of being an Elvis fan, to have his music generate new emotions is a tribute to the producer, musicians and technicians who worked on this collection.  "Fame and Fortune" brought a big smile; "Can't Help Falling In Love" brought teary eyes and "Fever" made me feel like I was sitting in Ricky Ricardo's smoke filled Tropicana Club!  More jazzy and bluesy, softening of background vocals at times when appropriate while at other times the background vocals were left where they should be.  Beautiful substitution of new instruments where background vocals were softened.  Nicely blended and augmented new instrumentation along with original instrumentation. If producers of Elvis' music would follow this pattern of adding new instrumentation to augment original recordings and balance the background vocals, I would be buying Elvis CD's like I was a teenager again.  Great Job!

Fever still had that bluesy sound but felt like I was hearing it in a dingy lit night club.  The song Suck On You didn't work for me. Overall it was fun to listen to and I would recommend it to Elvis fans.

current_product_reviews:
rating 5.0. The New Recordings presents Elvis as the King of Ballads This set showcases some of Presley's lesser known recordings which is a big part of the charm. The producers were quite thoughtful in the song selection and the modifications they made. These tracks are not startlingly different from the originals, but they tweak things enough to give the tracks a different feel. "Love Me Tender", which has been reimagined on two previous outings, is done very successfully here with female artist Cassie West. Some tracks reduce the work of The Jordanaires and while I don't consider this an improvement, it does give the song a different feel.

This Cd offers a lot of material. The overall feel of the collection is on the mellow side featuring lots of ballads. This recording is worth owning.
other_product_reviews: rating 3.0. Circulate is a bargain and weak CD release Neil Sedaka is mellow and in a easy listening mode with this release.  Audio fidelity is weak.....shrill and noisy.  Title track "Circulate" is a Sedaka/Greenfield original and the strongest track on this release.  "I Found My World In You" is another Sedaka/Greenfield original and offers a slow and mellow sentiment.  Sedaka is a great pop artist, but the quality on this Hallmark release is weak; and this is not Sedaka at his best.

A little change, but the avid Elvis fan will hear the difference right away, whereas the casual Elvis fan may not even notice it's been changed, unless playing the original version and this one back to back.

Overall, it's a good selection of songs, a little different then the usual "most popular" song lists we get from RCA or Sony.

rating 5.0. Buy This Album Elvis Fans - It's Your Duty! Excellent CD! The addition of strings, electric and slide guitars, sax, clarinet, flute, percussion, organ, etc on the various songs made long-time classics even more vibrant for today. After five decades of being an Elvis fan, to have his music generate new emotions is a tribute to the producer, musicians and technicians who worked on this collection.  "Fame and Fortune" brought a big smile; "Can't Help Falling In Love" brought teary eyes and "Fever" made me feel like I was sitting in Ricky Ricardo's smoke filled Tropicana Club!  More jazzy and bluesy, softening of background vocals at times when appropriate while at other times the background vocals were left where they should be.  Beautiful substitution of new instruments where background vocals were softened.  Nicely blended and augmented new instrumentation along with original instrumentation. If producers of Elvis' music would follow this pattern of adding new instrumentation to augment original recordings and balance the background vocals, I would be buying Elvis CD's like I was a teenager again.  Great Job!

rating 4.0. A New Interesting Take On The Familar This was a very interesting CD. While I'm used to the very modern techno productions done to Elvis' recordings this is different. Additional instrumentation here was used to enhance the recordings. For the most part, to my taste, some were slightly improved especially where strings were added, but I sometimes found the additional saxophone to be obtrusive. There were actually times where I could have done with less, not more enhancement. I realize that this is a very subjective and personal feeling since these are recordings I am very used to and very comfortable with as I have heard them in a certain way most of my life. The choice of songs was interesting as, for the most part, hits were avoided. Only 10 charted songs are here out of a total of 26 tracks and half of those are charted 'B' sides. The songs are from the 1956-62 era [in the public domain in Europe] and cover much ground musically.  Only one song was turned into a duet: "Love Me Tender" with Cassie West. In some cases the power of the Jordanaires' vocal support was diminished, and in addition to the aforementioned saxophone and strings, guitar, accordion, flute and other instruments have been added. Overall it's a mixed bag with much more positive than negative and is certainly worth the cost to listen to these recordings as you have never heard them before. The picture of an early sixties Elvis is very appropriate as only 2 songs are from the fifties.  I would like to see this production team take on another 26 of Elvis' songs and issue a Volume 2.

rating 5.0. The New Recordings presents Elvis as the King of Ballads This set showcases some of Presley's lesser known recordings which is a big part of the charm. The producers were quite thoughtful in the song selection and the modifications they made. These tracks are not startlingly different from the originals, but they tweak things enough to give the tracks a different feel. "Love Me Tender", which has been reimagined on two previous outings, is done very successfully here with female artist Cassie West. Some tracks reduce the work of The Jordanaires and while I don't consider this an improvement, it does give the song a different feel.

This Cd offers a lot of material. The overall feel of the collection is on the mellow side featuring lots of ballads. This recording is worth owning.

rating 5.0. Elvis rediscovered: an enchanting experience awaits the open-minded fan! A superb conceptualization crafted around Elvis' incredible voice (in his prime, here). Not knocking down FTD releases, as their CDs are also done with a lot of care, but how many alternate takes or live recordings of the same songs does one need to hear? This CD is quite different. As described by other reviewers,  the voice has been brought to the fore and tasteful musical background additions have been made to the original recordings. If one had never heard these songs before, it would be impossible to detect the enhancements. Being a lover of great saxophone, I particularly enjoyed the tracks where this beautiful instrument has been added (such as Fame & Fortune, for instance). But the whole CD is a delight. I understand diehard fans and purists may recoil at the thought of "messing" with Elvis' recordings, but this is so well done, and with so much respect for the artist. It only adds to the listening pleasure, in my opinion.  How about a volume 2? And soon!!!

rating 5.0. Enhanced rather than remixed Just received my copy of the New Recordings yesterday and, over all, I am very impressed! A great job was done enhancing these recordings! Notice I said "enhanced" rather than "remixed." Instead of completely redoing a song, only an instrument or two is added and, in some cases, it makes a world of difference. Mostly additional guitars, a saxophone, or slight orchestration is added. The extra guitars give some songs a more country feel, while the sax gives other tunes a more "blues" style. I do have one complaint. On "Fame and Fortune," there appears to be this strange static sound that is heard on the new sax accompaniment. I first thought it was in my speakers, but it was in the cd. I wonder if that sound is intentional or that it could not be helped due to the remastering? It's a little annoying, but nothing major. I love the fact that the temptation to do a "greatest hits" package (we get enough of them from RCA/Sony) was avoided. I've always loved the soft ballads that Elvis recorded in the early sixties and this cd has a lot of my favorite songs. The original masters were used and respected, unlike the "Viva Elvis" cd that was, in my opinion, overkill. I especially enjoy how some of the songs were extended making their running time longer. Purist may not like the fake duet on "Love Me Tender," but I think it sounds much better than the duet on the "Viva Elvis" cd. I think even die hard purist will find something to like on this cd. This would be a great way to introduce Elvis to the younger generation. Great cd again! Hope there will be a vol 2!!!

rating 5.0. ELVIS REFRESHED I managed to obtain my copy of this CD from the USA as it is not now available in the UK. We have many favourites here plus a fewer lesser known recordings that have been `refreshed` by changing some of the backing instrumentation, and in a few cases the backing singers (Jordanaires) have been deleted. "Love Me Tender" becomes a duet, I, personally still prefer the original, and "Fever" now has had more instruments added to its detriment.

Overall, however, this is a very good CD and stands well alongside the original recordings. The producer of this collection,Larry Jordan, also did a similar thing to Jim Reeves recently.

rating 2.0. Little compelling beyond Elvis' voice Meh. On one hand, it's Elvis, so how bad can it be? On the other hand, adding some dated sax to a collection of mostly obscure 1960's songs doesn't exactly make them sound fresh. In fact, in many cases, the songs actually sound more dated after the re-producing effort. In other cases, since the songs were not exactly standards to begin with, I was left wondering what exactly was so "new?" Some songs survived this treatment and were OK, but at the end of the day, there's little compelling about this effort. And by the time you hear the exact same piano treatment on "Something Blue" you get a sense that the producer just plain ran out of ideas. To paraphrase, "Something borrowed were those songs my ears knew. And that's why this CD makes me blue."

rating 3.0. 5 Stars For The Technology, 2 For The Song Selection (Read "Comments" Section) With the very honourable exception of "Reconsider, Baby", long recognized by discriminating Elvis fans as among his very greatest recordings, this is a pretty lame song selection. Yes, Elvis is in fine vocal form throughout, but most of the songs are second rate. Even such top hits as "Stuck On You", "She's Not You" and "Good Luck Charm" are hardly first rate Presley material, though they are better listening experiences here than they were originally. With so many great recordings of better material available, such as "Any Day Now" and "Stranger In My Own Home Town", to name just 2 of the songs from the legendary 1969 American Studios recordings brilliantly sung but seriously compromised by overdubs of voice, horns and strings begging for a new treatment worthy of the performances on the undoctored masters, why choose all these so so ballads? That said, and with the exception of some overly busy added tenor sax playing at times, this is a very interesting approach. Here's hoping the producer will extend his efforts into a more tasteful selection of material and really give us something revelatory.

rating 1.0. This CD goes against the wishes of Elvis. The producer's comments on the reviews ruin what little enjoyment the CD gives There is a blurb on all the websites advertising this CD that finally they have complied with Elvis's wishes and brought his vocals out in the forefront. However, this is completely wrong. The producer, Larry Jordan, will probably start arguing about this with me now (EDIT--just as I predicted, he has) in the comments to my review. (EDIT--now he has started asking Amazon to delete any and all comments on the reviews that make the CD look bad, see below. He is also personally attacking the taste and credibility of every single person who has posted a negative review on this site, in the review comments). If you read Guralnick's acclaimed Elvis biographies, you will see that Elvis complained repeatedly that Col. Parker tampered with his recordings to bring his voice out more. So, on the original albums Elvis thought his voice was too loud and he wanted to hear the backup singers more. On the New Recordings, Elvis would not be happy that his voice is now three times as loud as he thought it should be and the backup singers are even quieter.

The main thing that is wrong with this CD is that the versions are too close to the originals. I had no problem with "Viva Elvis," for instance, because they were doing different things with the songs. Here, they add just a few instruments here and there in the empty spaces, which ends up in most cases making the songs feel just a bit too cluttered. When you go back and hear the original albums, you begin to "hear" the New Recordings versions in your mind and ask "where is that corny saxophone? Isn't it supposed to play here?"

There are a handful of arrangements that ruined some of the songs that just should not have been tampered with. I am no purist. If something is different and it works, I like it. The worst part of this CD is the jazz saxophones that are just "doing their thing" all over the songs, sometimes even while Elvis is singing. It's a bit too much and it's like, "will you please stop?" Also, I thought the strings were fake, but I was wrong. There are actual string musicians. The string arrangements sometimes seem intrusive or badly placed.

(EDIT) Here is a more detailed breakdown of which songs don't work for me:

First, the "sax offenders":

So Glad You're Mine: The sax on this song is very out of tune (flat sounding) and annoying.
Stuck On You: An example of that busy sax just "doing it all over it."
I Gotta Know: Busy sax doing it even more and even worse this time.
Soldier Boy: More of the same..another busy sax doing its annoying thing all over the song.
Fame and Fortune: Not quite as bad as the others, but this "smooth jazz" sax sound is annoying.

And on to the rest:

Love Me Tender: A bad country duet with a woman.
Good Luck Charm: The guitar doesn't quite work.
Home Is Where The Heart Is: The flute doesn't belong here.
Can't Help Falling In Love: The string arrangement is cliche.
Anything That's Part of You: More cliche strings.
In My Way: The strings here are too busy and ruin the intimacy of the song.

I like the album even less after seeing all of the remarks from the album's producer and friends on all of these Amazon reviews. There is definitely a "campaign" at work here.

rating 5.0. The New Recordings presents Elvis as the King of Ballads This set showcases some of Presley's lesser known recordings which is a big part of the charm. The producers were quite thoughtful in the song selection and the modifications they made. These tracks are not startlingly different from the originals, but they tweak things enough to give the tracks a different feel. "Love Me Tender", which has been reimagined on two previous outings, is done very successfully here with female artist Cassie West. Some tracks reduce the work of The Jordanaires and while I don't consider this an improvement, it does give the song a different feel.

This Cd offers a lot of material. The overall feel of the collection is on the mellow side featuring lots of ballads. This recording is worth owning.

rating 5.0. A Grand Slam Amongst Single Hitters Even a purist as myself has fallen in love with this new cd. I never thought in my life that I would wish to hear Elvis's music any other way than how I have been for the past 40 years. But here it is! That breath of fresh air! Why no one had thought of releasing a cd of Elvis before in this manner is beyond me. Elvis has been given a new lease on life. Not that he really needed one. But we needed it! Twenty Six Elvis songs played as you have never heard them before. Each song lightly accented with either strings, a sax, new backing vocals and even bongos on a song such as "Fever". This producer knew not to over saturate each and every song with way too much new instrumentation. Just enough is added that is very enjoyable but never compromises the legend we know as Elvis. "I Can't Help Falling In Love" has never sounded better than it does on this disc.  With an added Nashville string arrangement, there is no doubt in my mind that if Elvis could go back and add it, he would. It is that beautiful. "Love Me Tender" is another one that has never sounded better. On this disc, Elvis is partnered with the sexy voice of Cassie West for a duet that is better and makes more sense that any of those Christmas duets released a few years ago. Another one that I don't believe has ever been released on a cd is "Home Is Where The Heart Is' which was featured on Elvis' Kid Galahad EP. With an added flute and string overlay, this is the one gem for those in search of this record's grail. Absolutely beautiful. "Reconsider Baby" is the track on this record where nothing has been added except for a remastering that this song has needed for 54 years. The fact that no new instrumentation was added, shows that this producer knew when to add or not add new instrumentation. A year ago I would have been a against something like this. But now that I have heard it, I realized that this was what was needed. A purist like myself needed to come out of the middle ages. I wish RCA/BMG/Sony would also. My Boy! My Boy!

rating 5.0. ALWAYS ELVIS I am listening to this cd for the sixth time as I write this.Elvis would be very proud of this one,the change of instruments,removal of some of the overpowering background vocals,and his voice out front and center makes this a cd that you can play over and over,while driving,working,or carrying on a conversation with someone.The song selection is great and brings back many fond memories.It is like hearing these songs for the first time and clearly demonstrates the greatness and perfection of the voice of Elvis Presley.If you want a really enjoyable Elvis cd,this would be the one.The mix of songs flow so well.I just can't say enough good things about this cd,just buy it and enjoy.It will bring to memory that Elvis was and is the greatest.

rating 4.0. A New Interesting Take On The Familar This was a very interesting CD. While I'm used to the very modern techno productions done to Elvis' recordings this is different. Additional instrumentation here was used to enhance the recordings. For the most part, to my taste, some were slightly improved especially where strings were added, but I sometimes found the additional saxophone to be obtrusive. There were actually times where I could have done with less, not more enhancement. I realize that this is a very subjective and personal feeling since these are recordings I am very used to and very comfortable with as I have heard them in a certain way most of my life. The choice of songs was interesting as, for the most part, hits were avoided. Only 10 charted songs are here out of a total of 26 tracks and half of those are charted 'B' sides. The songs are from the 1956-62 era [in the public domain in Europe] and cover much ground musically.  Only one song was turned into a duet: "Love Me Tender" with Cassie West. In some cases the power of the Jordanaires' vocal support was diminished, and in addition to the aforementioned saxophone and strings, guitar, accordion, flute and other instruments have been added. Overall it's a mixed bag with much more positive than negative and is certainly worth the cost to listen to these recordings as you have never heard them before. The picture of an early sixties Elvis is very appropriate as only 2 songs are from the fifties.  I would like to see this production team take on another 26 of Elvis' songs and issue a Volume 2.

rating 5.0. The Complete Collection This 3 CD set was well worth what I paid for it. I would say most all of Hank's best tunes are here on these 3 CD's. I would strongly recommend this to an avid Hank fan who is a collector of having most of his music, but not as a first time Hank listener as most of the songs are live or different versions of his major tunes. A whole disc of live songs mostly recorded at The Grand Ole Opry is included here and one disc has a lot of acoustic guitar solo work. Most of the sound quality is good; being that it was recorded over 50 years ago.

rating 4.0. Good value, but far from "complete" Any fan of Hank Williams' music has learned to view CDs called "Complete" with caution.  This three-disc set is no exception. Each disc has 20 songs: Greatest Hits, live recordings and solo demos.  The greatest hits are well chosen, but leave out essentials like "Ramblin' Man" and the wonderfully morose "Please Make Up Your Mind."  The live cuts, again, are well chosen, but are without any continuity or much explanation.  The last disc, solo demos (some later overdubbed with other musicians), is again a good mix of selections, but is far, far from "complete."  One booklet has a brief biography of Ol' Hank, while the other two booklets list just the songs and basic credits.  All of the above aside, "The Complete Collection" is a very good value (I paid less than $10.00) as an inexpensive introduction to the many facets of the incredibly prolific original King of Country Music.

rating 5.0. What's the most COMPLETE Collection? I ordered this "Complete Collection" set although I have another. What is appealing about this set? There are clear, definitive recordings across all of Hank Williams' too-brief career, as well as some radio spots.
There is no discernible enhancement or re-mixing that I'd detect immediately and would definitely NOT appreciate.

For those late to the groundbreaking all-electric style of the Drifting Cowboys, this Collection provides a great balance of early recordings without electric guitar, and later recordings with electric guitar. There's a dramatic difference.

I recommend the set because it does encompass many of the changes in the Hank Williams sound over a period of less than a decade. It makes it easier to appreciate the difference between 1963-1973 recordings of Hank Williams, Jr, and his later recordings (possible post-drug use)...I don't think that Hank III has evolved a signature style yet, just saying ))).

Otherwise, if you have no Hank Williams CDs, this is the one I'd recommend most highly.

rating 5.0. "Please Set the Transporter for the 1970s, Scotty!" I am an old-time musician and contemporary of many of the musicians in the original "Fat City String Band" (Berkeley) which evolved into the Highwoods String Band. Three albums were produced on the Rounder Label.

The announcement LIVE! brings back many memories for me. I would think that anyone who saw this band perform during its relatively short heyday will find magic here. Initially thought of as a revival of the Georgia twin-fiddle string bands of the 1920s and 1930s, the Highwoods Stringband (as written on the cover) was much more than that. The instrumentation was similar, but the aura and energy traveled far when Walt Koken and Bob Potts cranked up their fiddles. Koken is also known as a brilliant old-time clawhammer banjo virtuoso in his own right.

I have sought ANY film of this band in performance. If any reader knows of such a recording, please contact me through [ ... ] !

Watch the effect of this music on your children! Then, feel it yourself. There is nothing I've experienced that is closer to the joy of performing and interacting with the audience than this special group of musicians!

input: rating 5.0. CLAP LIKE OZZY!!! Finally in Jewel-Case Form!  I am NOT a fan of cheap, cardboard digipacks, and when I went to see Sabbath at the Forum in L.A., sure enough, "The End" E.P. was only sold as a digipack.  I still bought it, because of the new music on it and the live tracks...but I wasn't happy with the cheap cardboard.  So for all the collectors out there, we finally have a jewel-case version.  There's three original songs here leftover from the 13 sessions, along with four live tracks.  All good stuff.  Listen to it loud and CLAP LIKE OZZY!!!

rating 5.0. The Lost "13" Tracks Unearthed At Last! This "sold at concert venues ONLY" CD is quite the gem to the Black Sabbath fanatic like myself. When the reunited Sabbath (minus original drummer Bill Ward) got back together after Ronnie James Dio's passing and the end of Sabbath's Mark II's "[[ASIN:B001UREJWM Heaven & Hell]]", they could have put the phone book to music and we'd all come a runnin'. The resulting album 2013's "[[ASIN:B00BTHWFBK 13]]" was an amazing album considering that they hadn't been recording together for over twenty years and guitarist Tony Iommi's lymphoma illness. Though Bill Ward's absence was definitely felt, the songs truly tried to capture the spirit of the band's first four albums. Some songs even sounded like remakes of classic tunes like Black Sabbath, Planet Caravan, and War Pigs. However, when the album came out it was released in different versions, some with only eight tracks with deluxe versions had a total of 11 and 12. It was rumored that Ozzy, Geezer and Tony actually completed a total of sixteen songs for the record, and this CD prove that.

The first four tracks on the CD are the final four that never made it to any other version of "13" before it. Surprisingly, each of the tracks sound as mastered and completed as anything on "13" (even sounding better than the Best Buy only track "Naïveté In Black" which to me sounds demo-ish and somewhat incomplete). What's surprising is like the song "Methademic", a song that was on 13's bonus disc yet made it into their live set, the first track "Season Of The Dead" could have been one of Sabbath's next singles it's so good. The other three: Cry All Night, Take Me Home, and Isolated Man each have a uniqueness that have merit being on the final product as well, almost as if they planned for this release for the live shows, to leave four great tracks to sell as new a few years later. These songs are not throwaway to any Sabbath fan & must own to sit next to your copy of 13.

As if those tracks weren't enough, you get four live songs, all with incredible clarity and performance. To anyone else like me who has a copy of the live album/DVD "[[ASIN:B00FKIB3XA Live...Gathered In Their Masses]]", you already have a live version of Tracks 5 & 7, but the live tracks "Under The Sun" and "Age Of Reason" are brand new to the audio front. Under The Sun (originally on [[ASIN:B01H2ROWDE Volume 4]]) is special because I have never heard of them playing this live before (correct me if I'm wrong), nor at least it appearing on a bootleg. Age Of Reason didn't make "Masses" set either, so bonus points for that too. Make no mistake, even after all these years, nobody jams live like Sabbath, and these four tunes proves that.

In the end (pun), this is a great treat for Sabbheads. I would be fooling myself if somewhere down the road, these songs as well as all 16 tracks with the live ones, end up on some deluxe version a few years from now, but for now this is the only way to have a hard copy of them. Not to be ignored if you're either an Ozzy freak or Sabbath die-hard, this rare CD is quite the treat. Thanks for reading & thanks for Sabbath!

If you like Gary Moore, You owe it to yourself to have this album added to your collection . . . it's that good!!

rating 5.0. Love it! Unbelievable! I was not expecting it to come so soon! Thank you so much for the fast delivery. The album was not damaged; it was neatly packed and secured with bubble wrap. Photobook was awesome. I was expecting photos from their other 'day' outfits, but there weren't any. I find that to be a little bit of a bummer because obviously those pics were taken in the day so it would make sense if they were in the Day version. So that's on BigHit's part. I guess all the more reason to buy the Night version, haha!

Songs consists from both pt 1&2, with a couple new remixes and 3 new songs. Great job Bangtan, and kudos to BigHit for such an incredible group!

ARMY for life!

ARMY for life!

rating 4.0. Stompin'! Hard to believe now, but 2nd only to the Beatles in the mid-60's, these guys were the most popular. More than the Stones, Kinks,Animals, Who, etc. They appeared regularly on the Ed Sullivan Show and had numerous top-10 hits. They don't sound like any other band sounded (tho I once heard they modeled their sound on Gary "U.S." Bonds and that makes sense if you listen). Nothing profound, no insights into the universe, just absolutely stompin stompin stompin rock & roll. Buy it and put it on your ipod and play it at the beach this summer. You'll be glad you did!

rating 5.0. The real and genuine Dave Clark Five I loved the DC5's big radio hits when I was a kid in the sixties. I was six years old when "Glad All Over" knocked The Beatles' "I Want to Hold Your Hand" out of the top spot in the UK hit poll. It's hard to believe that DC5 was right there at the very beginning of the British Invasion! So many songs I remember hearing on the juke boxes at the bowling alleys, burger joints and public swimming pools my sister and I used to get dragged around in by our very young parents. So many hits I didn't know (then) belonged to a band I thought for many years was an American counter-revolutionary band trying to compete with the Brits! Face it, the DC5 sound as American as The Beach Boys! Anyway, the compilation will bring back lots and lots of memories to those who were young hipsters at the height of the Cold War. Even though all the DC5 songs are all pretty rudimentary, many of them bringing to mind chants and cheers like those made by the cheerleaders of that time, they were also songs that were incredibly catchy and enjoyable! Dave really knew what he was doing, as some of those old songs still sound just as good and, in some instances, fresh as they did when they were released nearly fifty years ago! (Wow! It's hard to believe this music is that old. Hot funk, cool punk. Even if it's old junk, it's still rock and roll to me!) "Glad All Over" will always be an anthem to raising the beer bottles high and rousting up a room full of sloppy drunks into a chorus of howling revelers echoing the words in the title of the song over and over again, each and every time Mike Smith calls them out with his teaser lines, "I'm feeling..." or "Oh, Baby I'm-uh...", repeated so frequently all through the song. "Glad All Over" rocks on this date just as hard as it rocked when it was recorded in 1963. And there are several other memorable tunes from a time when it was great to be young and listening to all that was changing in the world of music and in the music of the world. "Over And Over", "Look Before You Leap", "Bits And Pieces, "Because" and the hits just keep on rolling out of your mp3 player! This compilation makes a nice addition to your growing collection of remastered music from the analog days of old! Thanks for the great hits, DC5! And congratulations on your induction into the RNR HOF!
rating 5.0. Dave Clark Hits Loved the CD and enjoy being transported back to the 60's every time I listen to it!  The CD was reasonably priced and arrived quickly.

rating 5.0. Awesome British Invasion Band If you are a fan of the British Invasion era of Rock 'n' Roll the Dave Clark Five is a MUST HAVE group for your musical collection. They really rock 'n' roll! This CD is a good introduction to their top hits.

There are just a couple of low spots: Cut #22 is a medley of seven 1950s songs. Medleys should be reserved for bad lounge bands, not actual popular hit-making groups. The other low spot is the cut #28, a hippy-dippy slow song that is about a minute and a half too long. Can you spell "indulgent"?

Otherwise, this is a good collection of British Invasion rock 'n' roll from one of the best groups from the 1960s. Buy, listen, ENJOY! It's rock 'n' roll, music for dancin' and romancin'!

On this site you will see many reviews who are upset that the entire catalog of the DC5 is not available on CD. For those who are "completists" or huge DC5 fans, this one CD will not have all of their music, just 28 cuts of mostly their best known songs. You will have to look further for the albums, b-sides, and rarities. That is pretty much true for any "hits" package of any musical group, so I am a little baffled at the reviews of THIS CD that are unhappy it doesn't contain everything the DC5 recorded. This CD is what it is, a hits collection that covers what most folks remember of the DC5. Any one CD cannot contain it all. (This review is not meant to get into an argument with those folks who are unhappy with Dave Clark for not releasing more of his catalog.)

I would have liked to have the booklet.  But ~ that won't stop me from ordering again when I want the music.

rating 5.0. If Your Want the DC5 in Your CD Library - This is for you I am old enough to remember when the DC5 challenged the Beatles and Glad All Over topped the charts.  I have liked their contribution to the British Invasion canon of artists ever since.  While they didn't last as long as the Beatles, Stones, Who and were not as good, in my opinion, as even the Animals or the Kinks they are, in my opinion, worth having in my library of rock music.  This collection is, in my opinion, over priced and only about half the album is worth listening to but, there are no other options for collectors like me out there who want to have the DC5 included in their music library.  So, if after listening about half of the songs on this album, I usually eject it and then listen to something else or I replay the first half of the album again.  If you haven't heard the DC5 in a while or are too young to remember them and you like the British Invasion sounds of the 1960's then this album is for you.  In any case, you will be Glad All Over after hearing all of the DC5 classics that are included in this anthology.

rating 2.0. poor fidelity I thought they would have done a better job especially since Dave Clark supposedly had his hand on the mastering.  I remember the old greatest hits on vinyl sounding much better.  I'm going to see if I can sell it on CL to someone else.

rating 4.0. Not the Better Buy I think the Australian Import of the Dave Clark Five's Greatest Hits is the better buy, even just price wise being approx. $7. cheaper for even more total time than this package of "The Hits."  And one reviewer said "The Hits" package, remastered and produced by Dave Clark himself, should come with a 26-page booklet and the cover art is embossed, mine was not, with just a shiny one-sided piece of paper looking like an embossed cover.  Guess they ran out.  Half of "The Hits" package comes with latter-day covers of songs one probably has not heard even the last "Universal Love" being previously unreleased.  Still a good buy, just depends on what you want to hear from the Dave Clark Five.

This collection is, in my opinion, over priced and only about half the album is worth listening to but, there are no other options for collectors like me out there who want to have the DC5 included in their music library.  So, if after listening about half of the songs on this album, I usually eject it and then listen to something else or I replay the first half of the album again.  If you haven't heard the DC5 in a while or are too young to remember them and you like the British Invasion sounds of the 1960's then this album is for you.  In any case, you will be Glad All Over after hearing all of the DC5 classics that are included in this anthology.

rating 5.0. Essential to any rock'n'roll fan's collection I just received The Dave Clark Five The Hits CD in the mail today. Do yourself a favor, buy this CD while you still can. That said, this music has stood the test of time and then some. Dave Clark was a genius! These are STILL some of the best produced rock songs that were ever made! Think about that. rating 5.0. Great band that needs rediscovering Some of the greatest rock and roll of all time came from the 1960's, and what makes the decade so extraordinary is how the bands sounded coming in at the first and leaving by 1970. The Dave Clark Five started as the Dave Clark Quintet, the history going back to 1957, which is important as far as Beatles comparisons. While historians rightfully put The Beatles at the top of the heap from this amazing decade, we would be remiss (and wrong) to assume there weren't other outfits who were no clones at all, and doing equally fabulous work of their own. The U.S. had The Beach Boys and The Four Seasons - Britain of course gave us the fabled British Invasion, with tons of clones of the Beatles, but a few that were genuinely the real deal. Most folks will probably agree that The Yardbirds and The Dave Clark Five were the top of the line competitors to the Fab Four, although The Yardbirds, who boasted Eric Clapton, Jeff Beck and Jimmy Page as lead guitarists, were setting the stage for heavy music, especially after Jeff Beck split to form the Jeff Beck Group, and Page formed Led Zeppelin.
The DC5 were more pure rock and rollers, and they knew their stuff! This album was available just in the last few weeks for about $25, so I'm surprised the price has jumped up again. Dave Clark committed one of the biggest blunders in rock history when, after the band broke up in 1970, he saw fit not to release any material at all until after 2000, by which time LP's had worn out, there were probably very few cassettes, and they don't last either, and radio stations dropped them after their copies were too beat up to play. This meant a generation would come and go before the public got to hear them again, and by that time only those who were teens in the '60's even knew who the DC5 even were.
A splendid PBS documentary did raise awareness, including mine, and although I had heard them before, I had not paid much attention. I'm glad I did now, because they were great. Tons of hits here include "Glad All Over", "Any Way You Want It", "Sha Na Na Hey Kiss Him Goodbye", which is much tougher than the more watered down version usually played on the radio, and other great songs that bristled with energy and great playing throughout. It is interesting to speculate what may have happened had this amazing band continued and started forging new sounds. They were already tough as nails, so they could have been big time hard rockers with more guitars. Unfortunately we will never know.
But for those who love real genuine rock and roll at its best, and want to hear what other outfits from the Beatlemania years sounded like, you really owe it to yourself to check out the Dave Clark 5.

current_product_reviews: rating 5.0. could have been alive to enjoy it but that's another story The DC5 were huge in their day and churned out the hits, with their success lasting a bit longer in Britain than here. They should've made the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame sooner than they did so that Mike Smith, their superb vocalist, could have been alive to enjoy it but that's another story. The hits are here as well as some later songs and covers that you might not have heard before. The mystery is why Dave Clark is so stingy with releasing the entire catalog-surely the surviving band members and the families of all the members could use additional income.At any rate, they rocked and if you don't like most of this CD, you must be missing something.

rating 5.0. If you like her, buy the darn thing. I know, I know, I know that JM loathes her early stuff!!! This recording is at the earliest stages of her growth as a musician and artist..  But she is just lovely.  All the stuff about audio noise is there, but give it a rest!  I was expecting "bootleg" sonic quality (which can be atrocious), but it is quite listenable.  She comes off as a working musician, not "JONI MITCHELL"  that is to come later.  She is chatty, unaffected, and her sense of innocence just jumps off the recording.  She hasn't been called "Old Lady Of The Year" by Rolling Stone magazine, yet, and all the slings and arrows that goes with being in the public eye.  If only today's pop stars could go on a stage with just their instrument and be out-and-out entertaining.  The only way someone would not like this is if they are fighting with all of their might not to be JM fans, and they know who they are.  Leave your "hate" at the door. Joni's in the house!

rating 5.0. A Very Pleasant Surprise! I was so surprised to find this. Joni has released 3 live collections in her cannon, but this and a few others catch her in her innocence, vulnerable in front of a small audience, not counting those listening live on the radio. Her conversation between songs shows an open and honest young woman, months after the release of her first album and before she recorded studio versions of some of her best known works. Both Sides Now, The Circle Game etc. The other thing that is showcased is her ability with the guitar.....a six string! Her voice, not as affected by her cigarette habit at this point, is at once lifting and light then down to the lower ranges which makes her sound older than she was at the time. The liner notes read that she wrote Both Sides Now in 1966. When you think about the lyrics, it's comparable to Dylan's Blowin' In The Wind at the same age.

OK, technical quality. Fair. The disc starts out in mid song. It fades out at another song only to come back in applause. BUT the performance itself trumps those trifles, even the uneven volume of the songs. This is valuable for it's historicity and the facet that it is just Joni and her guitar. That was all she ever needed!

rating 2.0. Interesting, but not that Enjoyable I have been a big fan of Joni's for 40+ years. Especially her earlier work up to Hejira and "Summer Lawns". She is a proven genius of the singer/song writer genre and almost invented the style with Dylan, Cohen, Taylor et.al. I got all the warnings about the sonic imperfection of this recording and decided to buy it anyway, because I am such a fan of hers. And I'm glad I did. But I am afraid that the sound is so bad that it will become little more than a curiosity, almost a museum piece, because of the frustrating lack of clarity. Having heard it once, I may play it a few times more, maybe to show a fellow Joni fan. But when I want to enjoy her music, I will just put on Miles of Aisles, Court and Spark, or any other of her great albums that fairly showcases her crystal clear voice and her excellent and original guitar and piano style. Her music is unique and special and deserves a true recording of all the words and emotions she labored to express. This CD does not provide that respect. Beware.

input:
rating 4.0. A soundtrack for daydreams Nine Inch Nails' Trent Reznor, infamous for long delays between albums, sure has been productive lately. 2007 saw Reznor release the critically acclaimed "Year Zero" and its' follow-up "Year Zero Remixed." In addition, last year Reznor produced Saul Williams' "The Inevitable Rise and Liberation of Niggy Tardust." Now, out of nowhere, with no advance notice, Reznor is back with NIN's sixth album, the instrumental "Ghosts I-IV."

"A soundtrack for daydreams," is how Reznor sums up the new album...and that, I feel, is the best way to appreciate the new NIN. Rather than listening to this album as background music while driving or doing housework, "Ghosts I-IV" is best appreciated with relaxed, yet concentrated listening. Lie in bed or on the sofa, relaxed, and immerse yourself with this CD. Just sort of daydream--meditate, think about whatever...and let "Ghosts I-IV" be the soundtrack...

And as the mood of the album changes, allow your mood to flow with the album...try to get lost in the music.

Some have commented that "Ghosts I-IV" sounds similar in style to the instrumental music from "The Fragile," (1999) but I don't know if it's all that accurate to say that. While the instrumental music from "the Fragile" sort of held the vocal compositions from that CD together like glue, "Ghosts I-IV" is a little more "out there." It can, therefore, be seen as a mosaic--of several different styles--piano compositions, industrial beats, the avant-garde--all mixed together, with no real dominant flavor overpowering the rest. And while the album is all-over-the-place, treading many territories, everything works; "Ghosts I-IV" takes so many twists-and-turns-its always interesting. At the same time, with almost two hours of music to absorb, "Ghosts I-IV" is best appreciated with repeated listens--to fully appreciate all its' rich textures and intricacy.

rating 3.0. Ghosts Last year, Nine Inch Nails bounced back from the artistic decay that was "With Teeth" with "Year Zero", a record that I believe to be Nine Inch Nails' artistic high point, a dense but never fractured album that featured some of his highest production and some of his sharpest songwritting.
Now, only a year later, Nine Inch Nails and their leader Trent Reznor, who for all intents and purposes is Nine Inch Nails, have returned with the nearly two hour "Ghosts I-IV", a set of atmospheric instrumentals.
Now, if you would have told me even two years ago that the words "prolific" and "Trent Reznor" would ever, EVER be mentioned in the same sentence I would have laughed at you. But apparently after getting sober and getting the awfulness of "With Teeth" out of his system the man is just that. A total of thirty six tracks, "Ghosts" is as sprawling in ambition as "The Fragile", even without the accompaniment of vocals.
In fact, this new, quicker working version of Trent Reznor works much better for the music than the one who would spend a good half a decade pain stakingly going over every note. Like "Year Zero" last year, there is an immediacy to "Ghosts" that previous NIN records lacked. Though the record is dense and layered, it is never overblown with needless tinkering and a million small little pops and bells and whistles that can be found on earlier NIN products.
It's also nice to see Reznor in a true collaborative spirit, as "Ghosts" is co-written by Atticus Ross and Alessandro Cortino, NIN's current keyboard player. The difference in perspective helps give this record a feel different of any previous Nails album.
And let's all be honest with each other: the weakest point to any Nine Inch Nails record has always been the lyrical skills of Reznor. Usually clumsy, occasionally inane, Reznor lets the music do the talking for him this time, which is a nice plus.
So why only three stars for this record? Well, because at times "Ghosts" feels like a collection of ideas rather than songs. Though there is nothing awful on this record, there is also very little that is extraordinary, and it is too long for it's own good. The mood swings of the record are all complimented well, but too many song build and build and build and just stop, rather than fully taking off and entering someplace different and more interesting.
In fact, the best place to listen to "Ghosts" is probably in your car, late at night, driving home, where it can sink into your head but you're never really paying close attention to it.
But "Ghosts" is worth the purchase for sure. It's price, and the quality of good, if rarely great, music, and the fact that after almost twenty years Trent Reznor actually sounds like he's enjoying the process of making music.
Hopefully this is a series that he will continue to work on for many years to come.

rating 4.0. An exploratory audioscape After twenty years on the scene, the artist wants to come out with something experimental. For most bands this is a signal that they're out of ideas and are about to collapse into radio nostalgia on way-back-weekends.

Not Trent Reznor. The Ghosts are truly something very new on a long-known but previously underdeveloped foundation. Consider "A Warm Place" off Downward Spiral or the opening to "Something I could never have" off Pretty Hate Machine. These were reminders that beneath (and even coexisting with) the screams and mechanistic vicissitudes that define industrial music, Trent Reznor is a consummate musician.

"Ghosts" is fully a harmonic successor to the atmospheric movements on the very underappreciated "The Fragile". Consider the 1999 album's "La Mer" or "Pilgrimage" as opposed to "Where is Everybody?" or "The Wretched". Not to say that NIN isn't atmospheric, but rather that there are definitely tracks with an intentional message as opposed to tracks that are more meant for the listener to get lost in. Ghosts is clearly the latter.

This is not an album you'll "get" in one sitting. It would fail miserably if it were so simplistic. Let it grow on you as you meditate through it.

rating 5.0. NIN IS THE BEST MUSICAL PROYECT OF THE WORLD Great experimental and intrumental album, Trent Reznor is a Genius and unique artist, all albums of NIN are master pieces..

rating 5.0. I'm Not Good with Words I'm not good with words, so I'm not going to try and attempt to tell you what this album sounds like when Trent Reznor already summed it up in four words: "a soundtrack for daydreams". I, really, have never heard anything like this. It puts me in a state of mind I've never experienced. It makes me think, really hard, about what? About nothing. Peaceful, instrumental, brain music.

This is, definitely, one of my favorite albums ever. I recommend you listen to 01 Ghosts I, 02 Ghosts I, 09 Ghosts I, 13 Ghosts II, 18 Ghosts II, 28 Ghosts IV, 31 Ghosts IV, and 34 Ghosts IV before you click off the page, they are my favorites from the album, and are unlike any music I've ever heard.

I know this review is short, but I'm not good with words, and this album makes the words for me, in a way, and it's lovely.

rating 4.0. You must be in the right mood for Ghosts - Wanted Album, Am a Curious Cat - Glad I waited before Review 2008 Release 36 Tracks - Wanted to know what this was about. "Curiosity Killed The Cat, but He Died Satisfied" - am glad to have this in my collection, some tracks are more interesting than others, but it can be hard core, no vocals, instruments, sounds, hey, it's Metal. 9 tracks of Ghost I, 1st starts with a Piano sound, Some tracks in II have Piano sound, Some tracks in III, (Track 24 has a interesting sound, drive to it, like this one,) Ghost IV closes it's sequence with some resolution.  This is NOT John Cage with "Prepared Piano" it might grow on you.  My beautiful yellow, green & blue parakeet seems to like some tracks, sings along track 11, 29 Ghosts IV - is it organized noise? Are you man enough for this Album set?  You decide.. I'm a long time fan of Frank Zappa, & The Mothers, but many of his compositions have "comedy relief along the way" like "Uncle Meat" which was complex, but at least I could whistle the main themes, but this NIN is much more of a challenge. Thanks to Professor Fischer at Pasadena City College in '70 for introducing me to so many different kinds, types of music, Gregorian Chants to "Sunken Cathedral", and the many beats, musical scales. I did get an A- on my Synthesizer paper, learned much as well. It was my "fun" class when I went to college after USAF in '70.  This is a long way from singing in Church on Sundays. *keep smiling* -30-

input:
rating 3.0. Wow ... Cool History Lesson If you're an Ev fan you need this one to round out your collection. There are some really early workings of "Imaginary" and "Whisper" that make this a precious find. "My Immortal" is on this disc too, but sounds essentially the same, minus the hard rock ending (more of an acoustic end).

In my opinion, there are three other songs on this CD "Field of Innocence," "Even in Death," and "Anywhere," plus the ones above, that make it worth the purchase price. I definitely recommend this one to Ev fans and think that most Rock fans would like it as well.

Dino Smith

Chattanooga, TN

rating 5.0. Met my expectations Packaging is fine, and you receive pretty much the same things you'd get if you purchased from a Korean distributor (photo-card of a member, little note from Big Hit about auditions). It's wrapped in plastic wrap that's pretty easy to remove, but the wrap does leave room for the album to get dented. It's a thick card-stock kind of material for the cover, but it is prone to getting marked or dented. I received a small poster alongside the album. Only qualm I have about the poster is the fact that it was folded, but that's what you'll get for a cheaper price on Amazon.

As for the music itself, I would have to say I'm biased in the term that I can't bring myself to hate any songs. That being said, I actually do like the majority of this album, so you're going to get a quick overview of the music.

Tracklist:

INTRO: Never Mind
- Suga is a god, and I love his rapping. Very emotional and inspirational to say the least.

RUN
- It gets stuck in your head, since it's quite catchy. Hobie's "Don't tell me bye bye" is my go-to when I want to annoy a friend after we bid each other adieu following a date or shopping trip.

Butterfly
- Love this song. It's a higher range than what I'm used to hearing from the guys; it's a soft, soothing kind of song with some great notes from Jimin.

Whalien
- "I'm a whale" is my only reason.

Ma City
- Jungkook shines in terms of high notes and adlibs in this song. Really enjoyed his "yeah" falsetto after Suga's verse. Would have liked more from V and Jin, but V's laugh at the end gives me a little smile.

뱁새
- I do crunches to this song. The hype is very real, and J-Hope does a marvelous job with the adlibs. It's just a very fun piece that also happens to share a nice message about expectations.

SKIT: One night in a strange city
- Had to look at a translation, but it's cute.

고엽
- Great song by Suga. Very nice vocals in the chorus. V's part right before Hobie's verse was beautiful, as was Hobie's rap.

OUTRO: House OF Cards
- This is a song that, if you were well-versed in the sport of pole-dancing, it would be the perfect accompaniment. Gives off a very sensual feel, but it is just a portion of the full song that is later released in HYYH part 3.

Love the pictures and messages in the physical album itself.

I wanted the blue version but I bought this version instead because It came with a poster. WHICH IT DID NOT!!! I may return It and ask for a the poster and extra photocards when I buy it again.
